coursHub.title: Music Theory Lessons
coursHub.subtitle: Level 1 — 9 lessons to master tonal harmony
coursHub.level1Desc: 9 lessons to build a solid foundation — from the origins of the scale to modulations. The essential base of all harmony.
coursHub.level2Desc: 7 lessons to expand your harmonic language — modes, extensions, jazz, counterpoint. Step outside the classical framework and explore new colors.
coursHub.level3Desc: 13 lessons to reach mastery — musical form, orchestration, analysis of great composers, composition in their styles and deep structural analysis.
coursHub.level4Desc: 3 lessons to explore 20th-century musical languages — impressionism, polytonality, quartal harmony and advanced Coltrane-style reharmonization.
coursHub.c23: Composing in the Style of the Masters
coursHub.d1: From the acoustic origins of the scale to intervals and their inversions.
coursHub.d2: Build a triad, add a seventh, master inversions.
coursHub.d3: The tritone as an engine of tension. The SD → D → T cycle and the II–V–I.
coursHub.d4: Musical punctuation: perfect cadence, plagal, deceptive, half cadence.
coursHub.d5: Minor mode, parallel borrowings, Neapolitan chord, chaconne bass.
coursHub.d6: From melody to accompaniment: analyze, choose chords, realize.
coursHub.d7: Secondary dominants, neighboring keys and chains of tonicizations.
coursHub.d8: Change key smoothly. The cadential 6/4 and the V7sus4.
coursHub.d9: Harmonic sequence, common tone, minorization, pedals and appoggiatura chords.
coursHub.d10: Ionian, Dorian, Phrygian, Lydian, Mixolydian, Aeolian, Locrian — seven colors, one scale.
coursHub.d11: 9th, 11th, 13th — stacking thirds beyond the 7th to enrich harmonic color.
coursHub.d12: Replace V7 with the chord a tritone lower — same tension, chromatic bass.
coursHub.d13: Fux's 5 species — note against note, eighth notes, ties, syncopations and embellishments.
coursHub.d14: Color each mode with its characteristic chords — Dorian, Phrygian, Lydian, Mixolydian, Aeolian.
coursHub.d15: Advanced II–V–I, extensions, harmonic rhythm, turnarounds and jazz blues.
coursHub.d16: Diatonic, tritone, modal borrowing and parallel harmonization — transform chords under an intact melody.
coursHub.d17: Motive, 4-step development, repetition techniques, antecedent-consequent period and large musical forms.
coursHub.d18: The 5 elements of motive, the paradox of repetition, and the 4 families of techniques — from harmony to rhythm.
coursHub.d19: The 4 instrument families, their ranges and roles — doublings, balance, registers and SATB distribution in the orchestra.
coursHub.d20: Bach, Mozart, Beethoven, Schubert, Chopin, Liszt, Berlioz, Tchaikovsky, Rachmaninov — harmonic signatures.
coursHub.d21: Debussy, Ravel, Stravinsky, Messiaen, Satie, Beatles, Radiohead, Morricone — from Impressionism to rock.
coursHub.d22: Transform a progression while preserving the melody — diatonic and tritone substitutions, modal borrowing, parallel harmonization.
coursHub.d23: Bach, Mozart, Chopin, Debussy, Jazz, Rock — identify and reproduce the harmonic signatures of the great composers.
coursHub.c26: DEM Harmonization: Given Bass and Given Soprano
coursHub.d24: It+6, Fr+6, Ger+6 — the three augmented sixths, their contrary-motion resolution, and their link to tritone substitution.
coursHub.d25: Chromatic lines, the Tristan chord, enharmonic modulation — tonality pushed to its expressive limits.
coursHub.d26: The two fundamental exercises of the DEM exam — 5-step method, guided exercises with detailed corrections.
coursHub.d27: Tonal hierarchies, harmonic prolongation, Schenkerian reduction — learning to see the deep structure of a work beyond its surface chords.
coursHub.d28: Binary, ternary, rondo, sonata form — anatomy of the great musical architectures with guided analysis of Mozart, Bach and Beethoven.
coursHub.d29: Baroque, Classical, Romantic, Impressionist — 5 historical periods, one melody harmonized 5 ways to reveal the evolution of musical language.
coursHub.c32: Advanced Jazz Extensions and Modern Reharmonization
coursHub.d30: Planing, whole-tone scale, octatonic, pentatonic and Messiaen modes — harmonic color as an autonomous language.
coursHub.d31: Superimposed tonalities (Stravinsky), quartal chords, the So What chord — beyond classical tonality.
coursHub.d32: b9, #9, #11, b13 — available tensions, Coltrane's Giant Steps, the thirds cycle and advanced substitutions.
coursHub.level5Desc: 5 specialized tracks at the summit of the curriculum — Fugue & Chorale, Film Harmony, Advanced Jazz, Impressionism, Advanced Analysis. Choose your specialization.
coursHub.c34: Composing for the Screen: Film Harmony
coursHub.c36: Debussy and Ravel Harmony: Impressionism in Depth
coursHub.c37: Advanced Analysis: Schenker and Motivic Analysis
coursHub.d33: Strict counterpoint, four-voice chorale rules, fugue anatomy — Bach and the summit of baroque counterpoint.
coursHub.d34: Harmonic emotion map, leitmotif and transformation — Morricone, Williams, Zimmer.
coursHub.d35: Advanced substitutions, chord scales and professional voicings — Miles Davis and Kind of Blue.
coursHub.d36: Whole-tone scale, planing, polychords, neoclassical modality — Debussy, Ravel, Satie.
coursHub.d37: Ursatz, Urlinie, motivic analysis, 4 reduction levels — Schenker and Beethoven's 5th.
coursHub.d38: Suspension, appoggiatura, neighbor tone, passing tone, escape tone, anticipation and pedal point — the melodic ornamentation of harmony.

cours4.title: Cadences and Progressions
cours4.subtitle: Harmonic punctuation — perfect, deceptive, plagal cadences and half-cadences.

quiz.perfect: Perfect — you've mastered cadences and progressions.
quiz.veryGood: Very good! A few cadence types to consolidate.
quiz.keepGoing: Keep practising — listen to the examples again and retry.

narrative.cadencesH2: Cadences: the punctuation of musical discourse
narrative.cadencesIntro: In tonal music, a cadence is a harmonic formula that closes a musical phrase. Like punctuation in language, it signals the level of conclusion — full stop, comma, question or surprise. The nature of the cadence determines whether the music closes, opens or bounces forward.
narrative.cadencesInfoBox: All cadences rest on the same engine: the dominant → tonic relationship. What distinguishes them is the resolution — expected, softened, absent or subverted.
narrative.cadencesClickHint: Click a cadence to hear it in C major.
narrative.cadenceParfaiteEffect: The most conclusive of all. It closes a phrase with absolute clarity — the full stop of musical discourse.
narrative.cadenceParfaiteAnalogy: The full stop of a sentence.
narrative.cadenceImparfaiteCondition: At least one of the two chords is inverted
narrative.cadenceImparfaiteEffect: The resolution to the tonic is felt, but it remains less assertive — a conclusion held back, nuanced.
narrative.cadenceImparfaiteAnalogy: A comma rather than a full stop.
narrative.cadenceRompueEffect: The dominant resolves to the sixth degree instead of the first. The ear is surprised — the phrase continues where it seemed about to end.
narrative.cadenceRompueAnalogy: A false trail — you expected the destination, but the path goes on.
narrative.cadencePlagaleEffect: The subdominant resolves directly to the tonic without passing through the dominant. A soft, solemn colour — typical of religious endings and the blues.
narrative.cadenceDemiEffect: The phrase ends on the dominant, leaving a question hanging. It calls for a continuation — it is a punctuation of expectation.
narrative.progressionsIntro: The fundamental structure SD → D → T is the skeleton of nearly every tonal progression. But music is not made of a single phrase — it unfolds, breathes, insists, surprises. Three techniques allow this skeleton to be varied and elaborated.
narrative.progressionsInfoBox: Whatever technique is used, the SD → D → T logic remains present in the background. It is never a broken framework — only stretched, repeated or displaced.
narrative.progressionsClickHint: Click a technique to hear it in C major.
narrative.progressionsWarnBox: The II–V–I (e.g. Dm → G7 → C) is the most important cadential progression in tonal music. It combines the subdominant extension (II), the dominant (V7) and the resolution (I) in three chords — the backbone of jazz and classical harmony.
narrative.techExtensionDesc: Chain several chords of the same function to give it more weight.
narrative.techExtensionDetail: A function — usually the subdominant — is prolonged by approaching it with a chord that naturally precedes it. The SD → D → T structure remains visible, but stretched in time. Ex: Dm → F → G7 → C (two SD chords before the dominant).
narrative.techPenduleDesc: Alternate between two functions to create an oscillation effect.
narrative.techPenduleDetail: We oscillate between the tonic and the subdominant (or tonic and dominant) for several bars before resolving. This rocking creates a stable rhythmic and harmonic energy, very common in intros or codas. Ex: C → F → C → F → G7 → C.
narrative.techCycleDesc: Each chord descends a fifth (or rises a fourth) within the scale.
narrative.techCycleDetail: The complete diatonic cycle in C major: C → F → Bdim → Em → Am → Dm → G7 → C. Each chord calls the next through a descending fifth motion — the most natural voice-leading in tonal harmony. The T → SD → D → T logic is embedded within it.
narrative.inversionsSubtitle: For each progression, choose the inversion that ensures the best voice leading.
narrative.maitreAnecdote: In his Symphony No. 94, known as the 'Surprise', Haydn uses an unexpected cadence to wake up his audience. After a long, gentle phrase, a fortissimo chord bursts in where the listener expected a quiet resolution. Haydn loved playing with expectations — for him, the cadence was not just a conclusion, it was a dramatic tool.
narrative.maitreLesson: The cadence is the punctuation of music: it can affirm, question or surprise.

cours5.title: Borrowings and Classic Harmonic Progressions
cours5.subtitle: Minor as a colour source — borrowings, circle of fifths, chaconne and Neapolitan chord.

quiz.perfect: Perfect! You've mastered borrowings and classic progressions.
quiz.veryGood: Very good! A few nuances to consolidate.
quiz.keepGoing: Keep practising — listen to the progressions and retry.

narrative.mineurBody1:
The natural minor scale derives from the major scale — it is built starting from its 6th degree.
            But it has a problem: its tritone is not functional. It does not push toward the tonic,
            which deprives the dominant chord of its characteristic tension.
narrative.mineurWarnBox: In C natural minor, the 5th degree yields Gm (G–B♭–D) — a minor chord with no tension. Without the leading tone B natural, there is no strong resolution to Cm.
narrative.mineurBody2: The solution: the harmonic minor scale, which raises the 7th degree by a half step. In C minor, B♭ becomes B natural. The dominant chord becomes G7 — with the tritone B–F resolving to C–E. The leading tone is restored.
narrative.mineurInfoBox: Practical rule: we use the harmonic minor to build chords on all degrees except the 1st and 3rd, where we prefer the natural minor to preserve their stability.
narrative.mineurRowNaturellePb: No leading tone, Gm instead of G7
narrative.mineurClickDeg: Click a degree to hear it.
narrative.mineurDegVG7Desc: Dominant with tritone. Calls for resolution.
narrative.mineurDegVIAbDesc: Submediant. Soft color, often in a deceptive cadence.
narrative.mineurDegVIIBdimDesc: Diminished leading tone. Strong tension toward Cm.
narrative.empruntsBody1: C major and C minor are homonyms — same tonic, different mode. They share exactly the same functional tritone (F–B), meaning their chords fulfill the same functions. One can therefore borrow a chord from C minor into C major (and vice versa) without breaking the harmonic logic.
narrative.empruntsInfoBox: Borrowing rule: a borrowed chord must have the same function as the chord it replaces. This is verified using the functional tritone of the home key.
narrative.empruntsClickEmprunt: Click a borrowed chord to hear it and see its applications.
narrative.empruntsCmDesc: The minor tonic in a major context. Very expressive — the flat third creates an immediate color shift.
narrative.empruntsCmUsage: Final cadence on minor I, melancholic surprise effect.
narrative.empruntsFmDesc: The minor subdominant. One of the most common borrowings — dark, romantic color.
narrative.empruntsFmUsage: IV minor → I (minor plagal cadence). Very common in classical music and rock.
narrative.empruntsAbDesc: The major 6th degree of C minor. Replaces Am, bringing a brighter and unexpected color.
narrative.empruntsAbUsage: Substitute for Am in progressions. Widely used in pop and rock.
narrative.empruntsEbDesc: The 3rd degree of C minor. Replaces Em with a broader, more massive color.
narrative.empruntsTableTitle: Functions in C major and C minor
narrative.empruntsWarnBox: C major and C minor share the same dominant: G7 and Bdim. It is the common F–B tritone that unifies them — one can move from one to the other without changing the dominant.
narrative.classiquesBody1:
Some progressions have endured through the centuries because they combine harmonic logic
            with melodic beauty in the bass. These three models underpin hundreds of pieces —
            from baroque to jazz, rock, and pop.
narrative.classiqueCycleSubtitle: The most natural sequence in tonal harmony
narrative.classiqueCycleDesc: Each chord descends by a fifth (or ascends by a fourth) from the previous one. In minor, the full cycle traverses all degrees with natural fluency. It is the backbone of Autumn Leaves, many jazz standards, and baroque music.
narrative.classiqueCycleDetail: In C minor: Cm → Fm7 → Bdim7 → EbMaj7 → AbMaj7 → Dm7b5 → G7 → Cm. Each chord calls the next through a fifth attraction — the most natural movement in tonal harmony.
narrative.classiqueChaconneDesc: A bass descends by step or by thirds from the tonic to the dominant, where it pauses before starting again. This model serves as the foundation for a series of variations. It appears in Purcell, Bach, Handel — and in countless modern pieces.
narrative.classiqueChaconneDetail: Model in C minor: Cm → Gm/Bb → Fm/Ab → G7. The bass descends C → B♭ → A♭ → G, creating a melodic line in the low register. Note: Gm uses the natural minor scale here.
narrative.classiqueNapolitainDesc: The lowered 2nd degree (♭II), presented as a major chord and generally in first inversion. In C minor, this is Db/F (D♭ major with F in the bass). Its function is subdominant — it prepares the dominant with a uniquely dramatic tension.
narrative.classiqueNapolitainDetail: The half step between the ♭II and the tonic (D♭ → C) carries a particular expressiveness — a symbol of romantic pathos. It appears in Beethoven, Chopin, and film music. Classic pattern: ♭II6 → V7 → I.
narrative.progressionsBody1: For each progression, choose the inversion that ensures the smoothest voice leading.
narrative.maitreCardAnecdote: Chopin had an extraordinary ability to 'borrow' minor chords in a major key to instantly shift the emotional color of a phrase. His contemporaries described the effect as a cloud passing before the sun — in a single chord, the atmosphere shifted from bright to stormy, without ever leaving the key.
narrative.maitreCardLesson: We don't change key — we temporarily change the lighting.

cours6.title: Building a Harmonisation
cours6.subtitle: From melody to chord — identify the tonal centre, distinguish non-chord tones, build the harmonic skeleton and choose an accompaniment.

quiz.perfect: Perfect — you've mastered the basics of harmonisation.
quiz.veryGood: Very good! A few points to consolidate.
quiz.keepGoing: Keep exploring the sections — retry when ready.

narrative.maitreAnecdote: When the Paris Conservatoire jury criticized his unconventional harmonizations, Debussy replied: 'My only rule is my own pleasure.' He refused to treat harmony rules as immutable laws — for him, they were starting points, not limits.
narrative.maitreLesson: Harmonization rules are guides, but the ear remains the final judge.
narrative.tonaIntro: Before choosing a single chord, the first question is: in what tonal landscape does the melody move? Tonality is a force of attraction — the melody shows its preferences, its resting points, its poles of repose. Several clues help identify them.
narrative.tonaInfo: There is often no absolute certainty. Harmonization is an art of interpretation: one proposes the most coherent and natural reading for the ear.
narrative.tonaClickHint: Click a clue to learn more.
narrative.tonaStep1D: Key signature, leading tone, stable points, final cadence, accidental alterations.
narrative.tonaStep2D: Isolate the harmonic structure from ornaments — passing notes, neighbor notes, suspensions, etc.
narrative.tonaStep3D: Find the diatonic chords containing the real notes. Verify T–SD–D–T logic.
narrative.tonaStep4D: Realize the progression in 3–4 voices following voice-leading rules.
narrative.tonaStep5D: Choose the style that matches the melodic tension curve.
narrative.tonaWarn: Common mistake: wanting to harmonize every note with a different chord. Result: chaotic harmonic rhythm. Most notes are non-chord tones — they don't need their own chord.
narrative.indice0Desc: It indicates the probable keys — the major scale and its relative minor. Two flats → B♭ major or G minor.
narrative.indice1Desc: A half-step ascending toward a stable note = probable leading tone → tonic. The leading tone is the harmonic 7th degree.
narrative.indice2Desc: Notes held or repeated on strong beats often correspond to the 1st or 6th degree — natural poles of attraction.
narrative.indice3Desc: The end of a phrase often reveals the true tonal center — the 7th ascending to the 1st, or a V7 resolving.
narrative.indice4Desc: Momentary → probable borrowing. Prolonged → possible modulation. The artificial leading tone creates a tonicization.
narrative.etranIntro: A melody often contains notes that ornament the chord without belonging to it — these are non-chord tones. Identifying them is essential to harmonize without an excess of chords.
narrative.etranTip: Golden rule: if a note can be explained as a non-chord tone relative to a neighboring chord, no new chord is needed. Keep the current chord.
narrative.etranClickHint: Click a category to see its definition and example.
narrative.ne0Def: Connects two real notes by stepwise motion on a weak beat.
narrative.ne0Exemple: In C–D–E in C major: D is a passing tone between C and E.
narrative.ne0Regles: Always stepwise. Generally on a weak beat.
narrative.ne1Def: Non-chord tone surrounded by two identical notes — it leaves and returns to the same real note.
narrative.ne1Exemple: C–D–C over chord C: D is an upper neighbor of C.
narrative.ne1Regles: Stepwise motion in both directions. The ornament par excellence.
narrative.ne2Def: Note held over from the previous beat, becoming dissonant over the new chord, then resolved.
narrative.ne2Exemple: F held during G7 → F is a suspension (4–3) that descends to E over C.
narrative.ne2Regles: Preparation → Dissonance → Resolution. Always on a strong beat.
narrative.ne3Def: Note of the next chord introduced before that chord arrives.
narrative.ne3Exemple: E played at the end of V7 before C arrives — E anticipates the third of C.
narrative.ne3Regles: Generally on a weak beat. No resolution needed — it is already the correct note.
narrative.ne4Def: Non-chord tone on a strong beat, resolved by stepwise motion.
narrative.ne4Exemple: D on a strong beat during C major → resolves to C or E.
narrative.ne4Regles: On a strong beat. Stepwise resolution ascending or descending. Very expressive.
narrative.ne5Def: Non-chord tone approached by step, resolved by a leap.
narrative.ne5Exemple: C → D (stepwise) → resolution by leap to B. D is the escape tone.
narrative.ne5Regles: Stepwise arrival, disjunct departure. Rarer, element of surprise.
narrative.squelIntro: The harmonic skeleton is the 3- or 4-voice realization of the progression — before any stylistic choice. This is the decisive step: it tests the solidity of the harmonic structure.
narrative.squelCard0D: Second pillar of harmony. Solid at cadences, melodic between cadences. Inversions smooth out large leaps.
narrative.squelCard1D: Alto and tenor complete the chord. Prefer common tones and stepwise motion. Avoid crossings and overlaps.
narrative.squelCard2D: A 3-note chord in 4 voices requires a doubling. Rule: root. Exception: 7th degree → third. Never double the leading tone.
narrative.squelCard3D: Between bass and upper voices, contrary motion maximizes polyphonic independence and avoids parallels.
narrative.squelWarn: Absolute rules: The leading tone must rise to the tonic. The dominant seventh descends by step. Parallel fifths and octaves are forbidden.
narrative.squelTip: Key principle: good voice leading rests on the necessity of movements, not their multiplication. Keep common tones, move by step.
narrative.progFondamentalDesc: Fundamental T–SD–D–T progression. Root position at both cadences.
narrative.progRenverseDesc: I6 = C/E smooths the bass: C→E→F→G→C. Stepwise ascending melodic bass line.
narrative.progIiviDesc: Dm7 → G7: common tones. G7 → CMaj7: tritone F–B resolves to E–C.
narrative.accompIntro: The accompaniment is the expressive shaping of the harmonic skeleton. It supports, amplifies or anticipates the musical tension curve of the melody.
narrative.accompClickHint: Click a type to see its details.
narrative.accompInfo: The same harmonic skeleton can yield a nocturne (arpeggios), a chorale (counterpoint) or a jazz standard (comping) — depending on the chosen style. The accompaniment is an aesthetic choice as much as a technical one.
narrative.ac0Desc: Regular unfolding of the chord — ascending, descending or mixed. Creates a fluid continuity.
narrative.ac0Usage: Ballads, nocturnes, lyrical accompaniments. Ex: Clair de Lune.
narrative.ac1Desc: Irregular rhythmic fragments of the chord. Gives an inner breath and elasticity.
narrative.ac2Desc: Bass on the strong beat, chord on the weak beats — or the reverse.
narrative.ac2Tension: Stable — the bass anchors the rhythm.
narrative.ac3Desc: Punctual chords on off-beats — syncopated writing, lots of silence.
narrative.ac3Usage: Jazz, gospel, funk. Space is as important as notes.
narrative.ac4Desc: Each voice is a melodically independent motif. Requires mastery of voice leading.

cours7.title: Tonicisation
cours7.subtitle: Secondary dominants, neighbouring keys and chains — making a chord temporarily tonic.

quiz.perfect: Perfect — tonicisation holds no more secrets.
quiz.veryGood: Very good! A few nuances to consolidate.
quiz.keepGoing: Keep exploring — retry when ready.

narrative.maitreCardAnecdote: At the opening of his First Symphony, Beethoven does something scandalous for the time: instead of starting with a C major chord, he opens with a dominant seventh chord that resolves to F major. For two measures, the listener believes they are in F. This is a tonicization of the IV degree from the very first note — a harmonic coup d'état that immediately fails.
narrative.maitreCardLesson: Tonicization is a harmonic optical illusion: a new king is installed, but immediately dethroned.
narrative.principeP1: Tonicization means treating a chord — just for an instant — as a temporary tonic, by preceding it with its own dominant (and optionally its subdominant). It is another form of borrowing: instead of replacing a chord with another, we prepare it using the chords of its own key.
narrative.principeInfoDef: Simple definition: any chord in a progression can be treated as a temporary tonic. It can therefore be prepared with its own dominant — called a secondary dominant.
narrative.principeP2: Tonicization differs from modulation: we never leave the main key. The secondary dominant is a passing color — after it, we naturally return to the progression.
narrative.canBeTonicizedDesc: Any diatonic chord except the main tonic and diminished chords.
narrative.cannotBeTonicizedDesc: The main tonic (already the tonic) and diminished chords (too unstable to assume the tonic function).
narrative.principeExH3: Fundamental example: tonicizing G (V of C)
narrative.principeExP: In C major, G7 is the dominant. We can reinforce its arrival by preceding it with its own dominant — D7. Instead of C – G7 – C, we write C – D7 – G7 – C.
narrative.principeProgLabel: Progression: C – D7 – G7 – C (V/V – V – I)
narrative.principeProgNote: D7 contains F# (F-sharp) — the artificial leading tone of G. F# rises a half step to G. Without F#, G would lack this reinforcement.
narrative.principeWarn: Note: tonicization is not modulation. After D7–G7, we return to C — the tonal center has never changed. D7 is a passing color, not a new center.
narrative.principeSimplP: In practice, we can bypass the subdominant and move directly to the secondary dominant. Instead of SD/X → V/X → X, we simply write V/X → X. V/V can even replace a subdominant chord in the progression.
narrative.secondairesP1: Each diatonic degree (except I and VII°) can be tonicized. Its secondary dominant is calculated by going up 5 degrees from the target chord and playing a dominant 7 chord.
narrative.secondairesTip: Quick calculation: to tonicize X, count 5 notes from X (inclusive) and play an X7 chord. E.g.: for Dm → D E F G A = A7. For Am → A B C D E = E7.
narrative.secondairesClickHint: Click on a degree to see its secondary dominant and hear it.
narrative.voisinsH2: Closely Related Keys and Secondary Functions
narrative.voisinsP1: The closely related keys of a tonality are those that share at least 6 notes with it — they are the keys of the 6 diatonic degrees. Each related key corresponds to a scale degree, and its own functional chords (dominant and subdominant) become the secondary dominants and subdominants.
narrative.voisinsInfo: In C major, the closely related keys are: D minor (II), E minor (III), F major (IV), G major (V), A minor (VI). The VII° (Bdim) cannot be tonicized.
narrative.voisinsClickHint: Click on a related key to see its secondary chords.
narrative.voisinsWarn: Reminder: in a tonicization, the reference tritone is that of the temporary key (the related key), not the main key. SD and D functions are analyzed locally.
narrative.chainesP1: Since any chord can be tonicized, we can also tonicize a tonicization — applying a secondary dominant to an already-secondary chord. This creates a chain of secondary dominants, each link reinforcing the tension toward the final resolution.
narrative.chainesInfo: V/V/V → V/V → V → I: three nested levels of tonicization. In C major: A7 → D7 → G7 → C. Each chord is the dominant of the next — tension accumulates over three steps.
narrative.chainesEx1Desc: D7 tonicizes G7 before the final resolution. The double dominant reinforces the cadence.
narrative.chainesEx2Label: Double chain: V/V/V → V/V → V → I
narrative.chainesEx2Desc: A7 → D7 → G7 → C. Three levels of dominants. Maximum tension before C.
narrative.chainesEx3Desc: A7 tonicizes Dm (V/II). Dm resumes its usual SD function before the final cadence.
narrative.chainesBluesP: In the 12-bar blues, the tonic is played as I7 (dominant chord) — transforming it into V/IV, momentarily tonicizing the subdominant. C7 in C major contains B♭ (minor seventh) foreign to C major, but which announces F (IV) whose third is A — a colorfully resolved motion.
narrative.chainesTip: Combination possible: in a tonicization, all rules coexist. You can combine diatonic substitutions, parallel-mode borrowing, Neapolitan — provided you reference the tritone of the temporary key.
narrative.inversionP: For each progression, choose the inversion that ensures the best voice leading.

cours8.title: Modulation by Pivot Chord
cours8.subtitle: Changing key smoothly — the pivot chord, cadential 6/4 and V7sus4.

quiz.perfect: Perfect — pivot chord modulation holds no more secrets.
quiz.veryGood: Very good! A few nuances to consolidate.
quiz.keepGoing: Listen to the progressions again and retry.

narrative.logiqueIntro: Modulation is fundamentally different from borrowing or tonicization. It is no longer a mere play of colors — it is a genuine journey. When a composer modulates, the reference tonality changes. The harmonic center of gravity shifts durably.
narrative.logiqueInfoBox:
Key distinction:
Borrowing = a passing color (a foreign chord, immediately returning).
Tonicization = a momentary temporary tonic (a few chords, then back).
Modulation = a lasting journey — confirmed by a perfect cadence in the new center.
narrative.logiqueConditionsH3: The three conditions for a true modulation
narrative.logiqueStep1Desc: The departure tonality must fade — the new one can only establish itself once the old one has weakened.
narrative.logiqueStep2Desc: A perfect cadence (V–I, both in root position) in the new tonality — this is the strong acoustic signal.
narrative.logiqueStep3Desc: We remain in the new tonality long enough for the ear to adopt it as the new center. A single chord is not enough.
narrative.logiqueTonsVoisinsH3: Closely related keys — the preferred destinations
narrative.logiqueTonsVoisinsP: The closer two keys are (sharing many notes), the smoother the modulation and the more abundant the pivots. Closely related keys are the natural destinations for modulation.
narrative.pivotIntro: The pivot chord belongs simultaneously to the departure tonality and to the arrival tonality. It is played in the departure tonality with its original function, then reinterpreted with its new function in the arrival tonality. This dual membership ensures the fluidity of the transition.
narrative.pivotTipBox:
Method for finding a pivot:
(1) List the chords of the departure tonality.
(2) List the chords of the arrival tonality.
(3) Find the common chords.
(4) Choose the one with the most natural functions in both keys.
narrative.pivotAccordsH3: Common chords between C major and G major
narrative.pivotClickHint: Click on a chord to see its analysis in both keys.
narrative.pivotWarnBox: To avoid as pivot: the tonic of the departure key (too marked) and the tonic of the arrival key (prematurely reveals the destination). Prefer chords of intermediate function — VI or III.
narrative.pivotSchemaFootnote: Am: VI in C (secondary tonic) → II in G (subdominant). D7: first chord clearly in G (contains F#). G: perfect cadence confirmed.
narrative.pivotAccordCGNote: Too strongly marked as C's tonic — avoid as pivot.
narrative.pivotAccordEmNote: Good pivot: tonic in both. Discreet, non-cadential.
narrative.pivotAccordAmNote: Excellent pivot: naturally prepares D7 in G major.
narrative.pivotAccordGNote: Would work but G is the arrival tonic — too assertive.
narrative.outilsIntro: To firmly anchor the new tonality, composers have two powerful tools that prepare the dominant with heightened tension. They are like harmonic flags — they announce the arrival with brilliance.
narrative.outilsQuarteSixteP: The 1st degree played in second inversion — the fifth is in the bass. In C: C/G (G–C–E with G in the bass). The intervals above G are a sixth (E) and a fourth (C) — hence the name.
narrative.outilsDoublureNote: Doubling: always double the bass (G) in the cadential 6/4.
narrative.outilsSusP: The dominant 7 with a suspended fourth — the third is temporarily replaced by a fourth (often the tonic). In C: G7sus4 = G–C–D–F. C (fourth) then resolves to B (third): G7sus4 → G7.
narrative.outilsTipBox: Optimal combination: SD → I6/4 → V7sus4 → V7 → I. Each step adds a layer of tension before the final resolution. This sequence is the most elaborate cadence in classical tonal harmony.
narrative.outilsRow2Struct: V7 with fourth instead of third
narrative.exemplesIntro: Three pivot-chord modulations illustrating different tonal relationships — dominant, subdominant, and fifth-related modulation through diatonic degrees.
narrative.exemplesModCompleteFootnote: Am = pivot (VI/C = II/G) → D7 = V of G → G/D = cadential 6/4 (confirms G) → D7 – G = perfect cadence.
narrative.exemplesInfoBox: Analysis tip: in a score, look for the first accidental that does not belong to the key signature — it is often the artificial leading tone of the new key, and therefore the signal of the modulation.
narrative.inversionQuizP: For each progression, choose the inversion that ensures the best voice leading.
narrative.maitreCardAnecdote: In his 'Dissonance' quartets, Mozart uses pivot chords so subtle that musicians of the time believed them to be printing errors in the score. The transition from one key to another was so natural that the ear did not perceive the change — it only realized afterward that it had arrived in a new musical country.
narrative.maitreCardLesson: Modulation is a journey: the pivot chord is the invisible gateway to a new world.

cours9.title: Advanced Modulations and Harmonic Pedals
cours9.subtitle: Harmonic sequence, common note, minorisation, pedal points and appoggiatura chords.

quiz.perfect: Congratulations — Level 1 is complete!
quiz.veryGood: Very good! A few nuances to consolidate.
quiz.keepGoing: Keep exploring the sections and retry.

narrative.marcheIntro: A sequence is a musical pattern (harmonic or melodic) repeated at different scale degrees — as if the pattern were progressively shifted. There are two main families with very different effects.
narrative.marcheInfoCanon: Pachelbel's Canon: the most famous example of a diatonic sequence. The I–V pattern is transposed onto VI–III then IV–I, remaining in D major throughout.
narrative.marcheClickHint: Click to compare the two types.
narrative.notecomIntro: Instead of a common chord shared between two keys (pivot), here a single note serves as the link. This note belongs to the departure chord with one function, then is held while the harmony changes — it reappears in the new chord with a different function.
narrative.notecomTipContent:
Classic example: G major → Eb major with G at the soprano.
G = tonic of G (root) → G = third of Eb (Eb–G–Bb).
Same pitch, two different roles. The modulation is almost imperceptible.
narrative.notecomInfoContent: Where to place the common note? Preferably at the soprano — it is the most audible and guides the ear. In the bass, it creates more of a pedal effect. In inner voices, the effect is more subtle.
narrative.notecomTierceEx: E of C = third of C → third of Am or root of Em
narrative.notecomSeptiemeEx: Bb of C7 = seventh of C7 → root of Bb or third of Gm
narrative.notecomNeuviemeEx: D of C9 = ninth of C → third of Bb or root of Dm
narrative.notecomTreizièmeEx: A of C13 = thirteenth of C → root of Am or third of F
narrative.notecomEx1Label: G → Eb via G (third of Eb)
narrative.notecomEx1Desc: G = I of G → III of Eb. Harmony changes, G held at soprano.
narrative.notecomEx2Label: C → Am via E (I of Am)
narrative.notecomEx2Desc: E = III of C → I of Am. C becomes Am — modulation to the relative minor.
narrative.notecomEx3Label: C → F via F (IV of C)
narrative.notecomEx3Desc: F = IV of C → I of F. C7 (V/IV) confirms F as new centre.
narrative.notecomWarnContent: Modulation by common note is particularly effective for reaching distant keys (mediants, third relationships) where a diatonic pivot chord is hard to find.
narrative.minorIntro: Minorisation transforms a major chord into its minor homonym — the third is lowered by a semitone. This new minor chord can then be reinterpreted as a degree in another key, thereby initiating a modulation.
narrative.minorTipContent:
Fundamental example: C (C–E–G) → Cm (C–Eb–G)
Cm = IIm of Bb major → F7 (V of Bb) → Bb
By lowering a single semitone (E → Eb), we modulate from C to Bb.
narrative.minorRow1Desc: The most dramatic — the tonic itself darkens. Very expressive in Romantic codas.
narrative.minorRow2Desc: IVm is often just a colourful borrowing, not always a modulation. Dark colour on the subdominant.
narrative.minorRow3Vers: G minor or Gm is VI of Bb
narrative.minorRow3Desc: Less frequent but expressive — the reverse majorisation (Im→I) is also possible.
narrative.minorInfoContent: Common cases: IV major → IV minor (classic Romantic borrowing), I major → I minor (very expressive), V major → V minor (rare but effective as modal preparation).
narrative.pedalesIntro: A harmonic pedal is a note held (sustained or repeated) in one voice while the other voices change chords. It may belong to the passing chords — or be foreign to them, creating expressive dissonances resolved later.
narrative.pedalesClickHint: Click on a pedal type to see its details.
narrative.pedalesWarnContent: Origin of the term: the organ has a pedalboard — a keyboard played with the feet that holds the bass notes. The organist can sustain a note with a foot while both hands play freely. Hence the term 'organ pedal' (harmonic pedal).
narrative.appogIntro: An appoggiatura chord is the harmonic equivalent of the appoggiatura note — an entire chord foreign to the current function, inserted on a strong beat, which resolves to a real chord. The dissonance is brief but expressive.
narrative.appogInfoSchema: Functional schema: Foreign chord (strong beat, dissonant) → Real chord (stepwise resolution of voices)
narrative.appogDomToniqueH3: The dominant chord over the tonic — the classic case
narrative.appogDomToniqueTitle: G7/C — appoggiatura dominant over tonic bass
narrative.appogDomToniqueDesc: G7/C = G–B–D–F with C in the bass. The chord expresses dominant tension (G7), but the C in the bass prevents real resolution — C is the tonic, not G. It is a harmonic delay of the final resolution.
narrative.appogTipContent: Full sequence: Dm → G7/C → C or I6/4 → G7/C → C. The appoggiatura chord G7/C delays the resolution to C — the longer the tension is prolonged, the more satisfying the resolution.
narrative.appogSopranoDesc: The soprano pedal naturally creates appoggiatura chords: the held note at the soprano becomes foreign to the chords changing beneath it, creating successive dissonances resolved each time the harmony "meets" the pedal. This is the technique characteristic of Impressionist writing.
narrative.appogInfoUsage: Romantic usage: the codas of Beethoven, Brahms and Mahler often accumulate: SD → I6/4 → G7/C → C. Each step delays the final resolution — the cathartic effect is at its maximum when pure C finally arrives.
narrative.maitreCardAnecdote: In The Erlking, Schubert uses a pedal note at the piano — a low note obstinately repeated — to simulate the horse's obsessive gallop. Meanwhile the harmony goes wild, modulating into increasingly dark keys. The pedal stays fixed like an anchor while everything sinks around it.
narrative.maitreCardLesson: The pedal creates powerful psychological tension by staying fixed while everything moves around it.

cours1.title: Scale, Degrees and Intervals
cours1.subtitle: How 7 notes build an entire musical language.

quiz.perfect: Perfect — you've mastered the scale fundamentals.
quiz.veryGood: Very good! A few points to consolidate.
quiz.keepGoing: Keep practising — review the sections and retry.

narrative.maitreCardAnecdote: Walking past a forge, Pythagoras noticed that certain hammers produce harmonious sounds together. By measuring their weight, he discovered that harmony is a mathematical proportion: a hammer twice as heavy produces an octave, one and a half produces a fifth. Music is not a matter of taste — it is a matter of physics.
narrative.maitreCardLesson: The interval is the fundamental building block of music. A question of acoustics before being a question of aesthetics.
narrative.originesH2: Where do these 7 notes come from?
narrative.originesP1: The earliest Western music theories, notably those of the Greeks with Pythagoras, sought to build the scale on simple frequency ratios — sounds that naturally agree with the ear.
narrative.originesP2: The starting point is the octave: a note and its double in frequency sound identical at different pitches. The second fundamental consonance is the perfect fifth (ratio 3/2). By chaining six fifths starting from F:
narrative.originesP3: Bringing each note back within the same octave yields exactly 7 distinct pitches. Arranged in ascending pitch order, they form the natural diatonic scale.
narrative.originesInfoBox:
In C major: C – D – E – F – G – A – B.
The simplest scale: no accidentals, all the white keys of the piano.
narrative.originesP4: In the 11th century, Benedictine monk Guido d'Arezzo sought a more effective way to teach singing. He noticed that the first syllables of each verse of a hymn to Saint John the Baptist correspond to the successive notes of the scale:
narrative.originesP5: Si / B was added later (initials of Sancte Ioannes). In the 16th century, Ut became Do — a more open and singable syllable.
narrative.originesWarnBox: Two systems coexist: the Latin system (Do Ré Mi Fa Sol La Si) used in France, Italy and Spain, and the English system (C D E F G A B) used worldwide in jazz, pop and modern harmony. Harmonia uses English notation as the universal standard.
narrative.degresH2: The 7 degrees: each note has a role
narrative.degresP1: In a scale, each note holds a position called a degree. This is a relative concept: F is the 4th degree in C major, but the 1st degree in F major. The names of degrees each reflect a precise acoustic reality within the tonal system.
narrative.degresTip: Click on a degree to discover the origin of its name and its attractions.
narrative.degresInfoBox: Fundamental attractions: The strongest pair is B → C (7th to 1st) — only one semitone separates them. The second pair is F → E (4th to 3rd), particularly active during the resolution of the dominant. These two attractions are the engine of all tonal harmony.
narrative.tonsH2: The internal structure: tones and semitones
narrative.tonsP1: The 7 notes are not equally spaced. Between some there is a tone (2 semitones), between others only a semitone. This precise pattern gives the major scale its characteristic colour — it is identical regardless of the starting note.
narrative.tonsInfoBox:
Universal formula for the major scale: T – T – ½ – T – T – T – ½.
The two semitones always fall between degrees III–IV and VII–I.
narrative.tonsAccidentalNote: Without it, the pattern T–T–½–T–T–T–½ would not be maintained.
narrative.intervallesP1: An interval is the distance between two notes. It is defined by its name (how many degrees separate the two notes, endpoints included) and its quality (the exact number of semitones, which determines the tonal colour).
narrative.intervallesInfoBox: Inversion law: inverting an interval (raising the lower note by an octave) always yields an interval whose name adds up to 9 with the original (e.g. third 3 + sixth 6 = 9). The quality inverts: major ↔ minor, perfect ↔ perfect.
narrative.intervallesTip: Click on an interval to hear it, then its inversion.
narrative.tritonSelfNote: The tritone is its own inversion!

cours2.title: Chords
cours2.subtitle: Build a triad, enrich with a seventh, master inversions.

quiz.perfect: Perfect — you've mastered chords and their structures.
quiz.veryGood: Very good! A few nuances to consolidate.
quiz.keepGoing: Keep exploring — listen to the chords again and retry.

narrative.triadesBody: A chord is born from a simple idea: stacking thirds. Start from a root, move up by a third, then another. Two stacked thirds produce three pitches — a triad. What differentiates the four types is the size of each third.
narrative.gammeTitle: The 7 chords of the major scale
narrative.gammeBody: By building a triad on each scale degree — using only the notes of that scale — we get 7 chords, each with a distinct color and function.
narrative.gammeClickHint: Click a degree to see and hear the chord:
narrative.gammeInfoBox:
Fixed sequence in major scale:
I=Maj · II=min · III=min · IV=Maj · V=Maj · VI=min · VII=dim
narrative.tetradsBody: We enrich a triad by adding an extra third above the fifth. This 4th note — the seventh — deepens the chord's color without breaking its structure.
narrative.tetradsWarnBox: The V degree (G7) is the only dominant 7 chord (X7) in the scale. It contains the full tritone B–F.
narrative.renversementsBody: A chord always contains the same notes — but which note is placed in the bass changes the color. The lowest note determines the inversion. The notation uses a slash: C/E = C major with E in the bass.
narrative.renversementsInfoBox: Slash notation: C/E = C major chord with E in the bass. Always: chord name / bass note. Root position has no slash.
narrative.quizScoreGood: Very good! A few points left to consolidate.
narrative.quizScoreKeepGoing: Keep practising — re-read the sections and try again.
narrative.maitreAnecdote: It is said that Bach would get up at night to resolve on the piano a tritone left unresolved by his sons. The dissonant chord created such tension in his ear that he could not sleep without resolving it. For him, a chord was never a static note — it was a force in motion.
narrative.maitreLesson: A chord is not merely a stack of notes — it is a force seeking rest.
narrative.invDesc0: The root is in the bass. The chord sounds full, stable, grounded.
narrative.invDesc1: The third is in the bass. The chord sounds lighter, more open — smooths the bass line.
narrative.invDesc2: The fifth is in the bass. More unstable, often used as a passing chord.
narrative.invDesc3: The seventh is in the bass. Very suspended colour — pushes strongly toward resolution.

cours3.title: Tonal Functions and Voice Leading
cours3.subtitle: The tritone as tension engine, T/SD/D functions, and the rules of 4-voice counterpoint.

quiz.perfect: Perfect — the tritone and tonal functions hold no more secrets.
quiz.veryGood: Very good! A few nuances to consolidate.
quiz.keepGoing: Keep exploring — listen to the progressions again and retry.

narrative.tritonH2: The tritone — engine of harmonic tension
narrative.tritonP1: The tritone is the interval of 3 whole tones (6 semitones) that forms naturally between the 4th and 7th degrees of a major scale. In C major: F and B. Its acoustic instability is the engine of all tonal harmonic tension.
narrative.tritonInfoBox: In C major: the tritone forms between F (4th degree, subdominant) and B (7th degree, leading tone). Both notes appear in G7 — which is why G7 is the perfect dominant.
narrative.tritonAudioLabel: Listen to the tritone F–B in C major
narrative.tritonAudioDesc: In G7 → C: B rises to C (+½ tone, leading tone→tonic) and F falls to E (-½ tone, seventh→third). This double contrary motion is the tritone resolution.
narrative.tritonH3Why: Why does the tritone create tension?
narrative.tritonP2: The tritone divides the octave into two equal halves (6+6 semitones). This perfect symmetry deprives it of natural direction — it does not know where to go. The ear perceives this ambiguity as tension that must resolve.
narrative.tritonWarnBox: The tritone in C major is the functional key: the presence of both notes F and B in a chord determines its function. It organises all harmonic tension.
narrative.tritonH3Classification: Chord classification relative to the tritone
narrative.fonctionsP1: Every chord in a major scale fulfils a functional role determined by its relationship to the tritone. That role defines its behaviour in the progression — whether it creates rest, preparation, or tension.
narrative.fonctionsTipBox: Quick memo: F and B? → Dominant. F alone? → Subdominant. Neither? → Tonic.
narrative.fonctionsClickHint: Click a degree to hear it and see its analysis.
narrative.fonctionsInfoBox: Diatonic substitutions: two chords of the same function can replace each other. Am ↔ C (two tonics), Dm ↔ F (two subdominants). This flexibility allows harmonic variety without changing the functional logic.
narrative.fonctionsWarnBox: Em (3rd degree): a 'bastard' chord — it contains B (leading tone) but not F. Its function is ambiguous. Composers disagree on its role, and it is rarely used alone in a simple progression.
narrative.progressionsP1: The sequence SD → D → T is the backbone of tonal harmony. All progressions are variants or extensions of it. Cadences punctuate the musical discourse like punctuation marks.
narrative.progressionsClickHint: Click a progression to listen and see its analysis.
narrative.voixP1: Four-voice harmony (Soprano, Alto, Tenor, Bass) is not a stack of chords — it is a controlled polyphony where each voice has its own melodic logic. Voice leading is the art of moving these 4 lines simultaneously in a fluid and logical way.
narrative.voixClickHint: Click a rule to see its details.
narrative.voixTipBox: Key principle: good voice leading relies not on multiplying movements, but on their necessity. Keep common tones, move by step, and only move a voice when musically required.
narrative.voixH3Minimaliste: The minimalist model: II – V – I
narrative.voixRowSopranoMouvement: D holds, then rises to E (+whole tone)
narrative.voixRowAltoMouvement: F holds (7th of G7), falls to E (-½)
narrative.voixRowTenorMouvement: A falls to G (-whole tone), holds
narrative.voixRowBasseMouvement: D rises to G (+4th), falls to C (-5th)
narrative.maitreCardAnecdote: Brahms was obsessed with the purity of four-voice writing. He hunted parallel fifths and octaves as absolute offences against taste, marking his students' scores in red. He said that two voices moving together in fifths cease to exist as independent voices — they become one and the same colour.
narrative.maitreCardLesson: Voice leading is the art of making each melodic line independent and fluid.

cours10.title: Modes of the Major Scale
cours10.subtitle: Ionian, Dorian, Phrygian, Lydian, Mixolydian, Aeolian, Locrian — seven colors, one scale.

quiz.perfect: Perfect — you've mastered the modes!
quiz.veryGood: Well done! A few more listens and you'll have it.
quiz.keepGoing: Keep listening actively — the ear learns with practice!

narrative.modesH2: The 7 modes of the major scale
narrative.modesIntro: A mode is simply the major scale played from a different degree. In C major (C D E F G A B), if we start on D and play the same notes up to D, we get the Dorian mode. Each starting degree creates a unique harmonic color.
narrative.modesInfoBox: Fundamental principle: all modes share the same notes — it is the starting note (the tonic) that changes everything. By shifting the tonic, we change the intervals and therefore the emotional color.
narrative.modesClickHint: Click on a mode to hear it and see its details.
narrative.harmonieIntro: Each mode generates its own diatonic chords. The harmonic color of a mode comes as much from its chords as from its scale — it is the relationship between the tonic and the surrounding chords that creates the modal identity.
narrative.harmonieInfoBox: Modal vs tonal thinking: in tonal music, chords create tension and call for resolution (SD → D → T). In modal music, chords establish a color — they do not necessarily call for resolution. The Im → IV movement in Dorian is not a cadence, it is a colorful oscillation.
narrative.harmonieWarnBox: Modal vs Tonal: in tonal music, V7 → I is the fundamental resolution. In modal music, this resolution is avoided — progressions that maintain the modal color are preferred rather than resolving it. This is why modal music sometimes seems to "float" or "go in circles" — it is intentional.
narrative.quizFeedbackExcellent: Excellent — the modes hold no more secrets!
narrative.quizFeedbackGood: Good — a few more listening sessions and it will be perfect.
narrative.quizFeedbackKeepGoing: Keep listening to the modes actively — the ear learns!
narrative.maitreAnecdote: In 1959, Miles Davis recorded Kind of Blue — the best-selling jazz album of all time. Instead of chaining complex chords, he built each piece on a single mode. So What is entirely in D Dorian. Davis said: 'I wanted the musicians to think of a color, not a chord sequence.'
narrative.maitreLesson: A mode is not a scale with one different note — it is a harmonic color, a way of hearing the world.

cours12.title: Tritone Substitution
cours12.subtitle: Replace V7 with the chord a tritone away — same tension, chromatic bass line.

quiz.perfect: Excellent — tritone substitution holds no more secrets!
quiz.veryGood: Well done! A few more progressions to practice.
quiz.keepGoing: Keep comparing both resolutions by ear — that's how it clicks!

narrative.principeP1: In C major, G7 is the natural dominant — it resolves to C by a descending fifth. Tritone substitution replaces G7 with D♭7, located exactly a tritone lower (6 semitones). D♭7 resolves to C just as well, but the bass descends by a semitone (D♭→C) instead of a fifth leap.
narrative.principeInfo: Definition: tritone substitution (or "tri-sub") replaces any dominant V7 chord with the dominant a tritone lower. G7 → D♭7, D7 → A♭7, A7 → E♭7, E7 → B♭7, etc.
narrative.compCardStandardFeel: Strong, assertive — the classic authentic cadence
narrative.pourquoiH3: Why it works — the shared tritone
narrative.pourquoiP: G7 and D♭7 share exactly the same two guide tones (3rd and 7th), but their roles are reversed. It is this common tritone that allows both to resolve to C.
narrative.enharmoniqueNote: B = C♭ (enharmonic) · F = F — the 2 notes remain, the roles are swapped
narrative.tritonTip: The tritone resolution: B→C (ascending semitone) and F→E (descending semitone). These two movements converge toward the tonic and its 3rd — it is the attraction of the tritone toward the major third of the tonic, whether the bass is G or D♭.
narrative.applicationP1: Tritone substitution applies to any dominant 7th chord, whether it is the main V7 or a secondary dominant. Its most sought-after effect is the descending chromatic bass line.
narrative.ii251H3: II – V – I: standard vs substitution
narrative.bassLineInfo: The bass line D → D♭ → C is one of the most characteristic progressions in jazz. The pianist or bassist can build this chromatic descent in the bass voice, independently of the upper voices.
narrative.secondaryDomP: Any dominant 7th chord (including secondary dominants V7/X) can be substituted. In a progression with several dominants, a continuous chromatic descent can be created in the bass.
narrative.jazzExample1Title: Autumn Leaves — Miles Davis / Bill Evans
narrative.jazzExample1Body: The Am7♭5–D7–Gm progression is one of the most well-known in the jazz repertoire. With tritone sub: Am7♭5–A♭7–Gm. The bass descends A–A♭–G chromatically. Bill Evans and Miles Davis use this substitution in their iconic versions of Autumn Leaves.
narrative.jazzExample2Body: Monk, inventor of many substitutions, uses tritone subs in Round Midnight to enrich his own composition. Miles Davis and Gil Evans develop these ideas in their version with orchestral arrangement (1957). The chromatic richness of this piece owes much to substitutions.
narrative.jazzExample3Body: Giant Steps (1960) pushes the logic of substitution beyond the tritone: Coltrane divides the octave into 3 equal parts by major thirds (B → G → E♭), creating harmonic changes at an incredible speed. It is the ultimate extension of the substitution concept — no longer by tritone, but by major third.
narrative.limitWarn: Limitation of substitution: tritone substitution applies to dominant 7th chords only (unstable internal tritone). It does not apply to Maj7, minor 7 or half-diminished chords — these chords do not have the same type of tension to resolve.
narrative.maitreAnecdote: In 1959, Coltrane began composing Giant Steps. He showed his scores to his musicians during the recording session. Tommy Flanagan, a seasoned session pianist, had never seen it before. The result is audible on the album: Flanagan improvises cautiously, stunned by the speed of the harmonic changes — a modulation every two beats, by major thirds. Giant Steps was released in 1960 and redefined the limits of jazz harmony.
narrative.maitreLesson: Tritone substitution is discovering that a chord can resolve to two different tonics by sharing the same tritone. It is not magic — it is symmetry.
narrative.quizFeedbackExcellent: Excellent — tritone substitution holds no more secrets!
narrative.quizFeedbackGood: Good — a few more progressions to practice and it will be perfect.
narrative.quizFeedbackKeepGoing: Keep listening to both resolutions — the ear is trained through comparison!

cours14.title: Modal Harmonisation
cours14.subtitle: Colouring each mode with its characteristic chords — no mandatory tonal resolution.

quiz.perfect: Excellent — modal harmonisation holds no more secrets!
quiz.veryGood: Well done! A few more progressions to consolidate.
quiz.keepGoing: Keep comparing the modes by ear — it comes with practice!

narrative.principesP1: In tonal harmony, chords have functions: Tonic (T), Subdominant (SD), Dominant (D). The progression SD→D→T creates tension and resolution. In modal harmony, chords have colours: they oscillate around the tonic and sustain the colour of the mode — with no mandatory resolution.
narrative.infoBoxGoldenRule: Golden rule: to maintain the modal colour, regularly use the characteristic chord of the mode (the one containing the distinctive note) and avoid V7→I (which introduces the leading tone absent from the mode and creates a tonal resolution).
narrative.tonalDesc: I – IV – V7 – I: tension on G7, resolution to C.
narrative.modalDesc: Im – IV – Im – IV: coloured oscillation, no resolution.
narrative.warnBoxCommonTrap: Common pitfall: harmonising a modal melody with V7→I "by reflex". E.g. in D dorian, playing A7→Dm introduces C# (the leading tone absent from dorian) and immediately transforms the modal colour into classical harmonic minor.
narrative.modesP1: Each mode has its characteristic progression. Click on a mode to see its chords, hear the progression and understand what to avoid.
narrative.warnBoxLocrien: The Locrian mode: its tonic chord is diminished — almost impossible to use as a stable tonal centre. It is used instead as a punctual colour within a broader modal context.
narrative.maitreAnecdote: Debussy broke with tonal harmony by using medieval modes, pentatonic scales and parallel chords. In La Cathédrale engloutie (1910), blocks of chords move together without functional resolution. He would say: 'I hear colours, not functions.'
narrative.maitreLesson: Chords are not functions (T, SD, D) — they are colours. In modal music, we paint with chords rather than chaining them in a directed progression.

cours13.title: Two-Voice Counterpoint
cours13.subtitle: Fux's 5 species — note against note, passing notes, suspensions, florid counterpoint — the rules of two-voice motion.

quiz.perfect: Excellent — two-voice counterpoint holds no more secrets!
quiz.veryGood: Well done! A few rules still to consolidate.
quiz.keepGoing: Keep listening to the species — the ear learns with practice!

narrative.especesIntro: Fux organises the study of counterpoint into 5 progressive species. Each species introduces a new difficulty: first pure consonances, then passing notes, then suspensions, and finally free combination.
narrative.especesInfoBox: General principle: two-voice counterpoint is written above a cantus firmus — a reference melody in long notes. The counterpoint voice must be melodically interesting and harmonically correct with respect to the CF.
narrative.especesClickHint: Click on a species to hear it and see its rules.
narrative.especesRow3Fig: Neighbor notes, escape tones, passing notes
narrative.reglesIntro: Strict counterpoint rests on three pillars: the quality of intervals (consonances vs. dissonances), the type of motion between voices, and the rigorous treatment of each dissonance.
narrative.reglesWarnBox: Hidden fifths and octaves: even when voices reach a fifth or octave by direct motion (not parallel), this "hidden fifth" is forbidden between outer voices (soprano and bass) in the strict style. The two voices must not approach a perfect fifth or octave while moving in the same direction.
narrative.reglesSuspensionsIntro: A suspension unfolds in 3 mandatory steps: preparation → dissonance → resolution.
narrative.reglesSuspensionsInfoBox: Suspension cycle: 1. Preparation — the note is present as a consonance on the weak beat. 2. Dissonance — the note is held by a tie on the strong beat (the CF has moved, creating a dissonance). 3. Resolution — the dissonance descends by a conjunct step to a consonance.
narrative.reglesMelo1Detail: The line should be primarily stepwise — naturally singable. Leaps should remain rare and justified.
narrative.reglesMelo2Rule: After a large leap, return in the opposite direction
narrative.reglesMelo2Detail: A leap of a sixth, seventh, or octave should be followed by a gradual melodic return in the opposite direction.
narrative.reglesMelo3Detail: The highest point of the line should appear only once. Repeating it dilutes the dramatic effect.
narrative.reglesMelo4Rule: Avoid leaps of augmented or diminished intervals
narrative.reglesMelo4Detail: The augmented second, diminished fifth, and tritone are difficult to sing and create tonal ambiguities.
narrative.reglesMelo5Rule: Avoid prolonged repetitions of the same note
narrative.reglesMelo5Detail: A note repeated several times in a row stagnates the line and harms its melodic independence.
narrative.reglesMelo6Detail: When the upper voice drops below the lower voice (or vice versa), the melodic identities become entangled.
narrative.reglesTipBox: Fux's method: always start by mastering the 1st species in all modes before moving to the 2nd. Fux insists: each species is an indispensable tool. Florid counterpoint (5th species) is only accessible once the first 4 have been internalised intuitively.
narrative.quizFeedbackGreat: Excellent — Fux would be proud of you!
narrative.quizFeedbackGood: Good — a few more species to practise.
narrative.quizFeedbackKeepGoing: Keep practising the species one by one — the order matters!
narrative.maitreAnecdote: In 1725, Fux publishes the Gradus ad Parnassum ('Steps to Parnassus') in Vienna. He is 65 and suffering from gout. He writes it as a dialogue: Aloysius (his ideal, Palestrina) teaches Josephus (himself). This book would span the centuries: the young Haydn would study it alone in his room in Vienna. Mozart would use it to teach. Beethoven would copy entire passages into his notebooks.
narrative.maitreLesson: Writing two independent voices is harder than writing one complex voice. Each voice must have its own life — and yet, together, they must form a coherent harmony.

cours16.title: Reharmonisation
cours16.subtitle: Diatonic substitution, tritone substitution, modal borrowing, parallel harmonisation — transforming chords under an intact melody.

quiz.perfect: Excellent — reharmonisation holds no more secrets!
quiz.veryGood: Well done! A few more techniques to consolidate.
quiz.keepGoing: Keep comparing before/after versions by ear — that's how it clicks!

narrative.principleP1: Reharmonizing a progression means replacing some or all chords with others — while keeping the melody intact. The fundamental condition: each melodic note must be compatible with the new chord (root, third, fifth, seventh or extension).
narrative.principleInfoBox: Golden rule: before validating a substituted chord, verify that each melodic note is 1, 3, 5, 7 or an extension (9th, 11th, 13th) of that chord. If a note 'floats in the void', choose a different chord or technique.
narrative.principleWarnBox: Voice leading: a technically correct reharmonization can sound abrupt if inner voices make large leaps. Always prefer stepwise motion (half-step, whole step) for inner voices — a quality criterion as important as melodic compatibility.
narrative.techniqueDiatoniqueDescription: Two chords sharing at least two common notes can replace each other. In C: CMaj7 (C E G B) and Am7 (A C E G) share C, E and G. The melody stays compatible because its notes are present in both chords.
narrative.techniqueDiatoniqueTip: Replacing I with VI creates a 'soft deceptive resolution' — V7→VIm instead of V7→I. Less definitive, more expressive. Ubiquitous in Bill Evans and Keith Jarrett.
narrative.techniqueTritoniqueDescription: V7 is replaced by the chord a tritone (6 half-steps) away. G7 and Db7 share the same tritone (B–F / F–Cb) — same tension notes, inverted roles. The bass descends a half-step to I instead of a fifth.
narrative.techniqueTritoniqueTip: Bass Db→C (half-step) vs G→C (fifth). The chromatic motion is the acoustic signature of this substitution: same resolution tension, radically different sound.
narrative.techniqueModalDescription: A chord is borrowed from the parallel minor scale. In C major, Fm (IVm), AbMaj7 (bVI) or Bb7 (bVII) come from C minor. These unexpected flats momentarily darken the color — a poignant, introspective effect.
narrative.techniqueModalTip: FMaj7 → Fm is the most common borrowing. Ab (3rd of Fm) flattens the color. Evans uses it on Waltz for Debby to create expressive contrast within a bright section.
narrative.techniqueParalleleDescription: The same chord type moves chromatically under the melody. Inner voices all move in parallel by the same interval. An impressionist technique (Debussy, Ravel) adopted by jazz — Evans, Mehldau.
narrative.techniqueParalleleTip: Parallel chords are 'neutral' in themselves. It's the melody that creates harmonic interest and logic. Three consecutive chromatic Maj7 chords create an immediate impressionist effect.
narrative.applicationP1: Reharmonization can be applied block by block (changing one chord at a time) or completely (rebuilding the entire chord chart under the melody). Great jazz pianists combine all four techniques to create personal progressions.
narrative.compTritonsubNote: Bass D–Db–C: descending chromatic motion, bebop signature
narrative.compModalborrowNote: Fm introduces Ab — dark, poignant color very characteristic of Bill Evans
narrative.compExtensionsNote: Same function, richer color — Bill Evans' sonic signature
narrative.compDiatonicNote: Am7 shares C, E, G with CMaj7 — soft deceptive resolution
narrative.pianistEvansWorks: Waltz for Debby, Autumn Leaves, My Foolish Heart
narrative.pianistEvansStyle: Modal borrowings, extensions, discrete substitutions — 'signing' a standard without betraying it
narrative.pianistJarrettStyle: Free reharmonizations, parallel harmonization, expressive chromaticism
narrative.pianistMehldauStyle: Cross-genre reharmonization — pop melody, complex jazz changes with substitutions
narrative.pianistColtraneWorks: My Favorite Things, Body and Soul
narrative.pianistColtraneStyle: Complete reharmonization — entire chart replaced (modal or Coltrane cycle)
narrative.applicationBlocText: Change one chord at a time. Safer — you control the impact of each substitution. Bill Evans often works this way: a IVm here, a Db7 there.
narrative.applicationCompleteText: Rebuild the entire chart. More radical and creative. Coltrane transforms My Favorite Things into a completely new modal chart — melody intact.
narrative.applicationTipBox: Bill Evans' principle: 'never sacrifice voice leading'. Each chord change must allow inner voices to move by half-steps or whole steps. Great jazz progressions sound fluid precisely because inner voices move little — the melody does the expressive work.
narrative.maitreCardAnecdote: Evans plays Autumn Leaves on his first solo album (1956) and the listener recognizes the familiar melody — but the chords have changed. FMaj7 became Fm, G7 became Db7. Without altering a single note of Joseph Kosma's melody, Evans transformed a springtime standard into a melancholy nocturne.
narrative.maitreCardLesson: Reharmonization is not virtuosity — it's listening. Before substituting a chord, ask yourself: can the melody still be heard? Does the new chord say something the original could not?

cours15.title: Advanced Jazz Progressions
cours15.subtitle: Basic, extended and minor II–V–I, rhythmic harmony, turnarounds and jazz blues.

quiz.perfect: Excellent — jazz progressions hold no more secrets!
quiz.veryGood: Well done! A few more progressions to consolidate.
quiz.keepGoing: Keep listening actively — jazz is learned through the ear!

narrative.maitreAnecdote: Parker ('Bird') improvised in 1945 over Ko-Ko (Cherokee changes) at a speed that astonished his contemporaries. He simultaneously used tritone substitutions, secondary dominants, and b9/#9 extensions that no one had chained together so rapidly. Dizzy Gillespie said: 'He heard things we hadn't even heard yet.'
narrative.maitreLesson: II–V–I is not a formula — it's a space for exploration. The more you master its variations (extensions, substitutions, chains), the more colours you have at your disposal.
narrative.iiviIntro: The II–V–I is the fundamental harmonic cell of jazz. IIm7 prepares, V7 creates tension, IMaj7 resolves. This same structure exists in basic form, with extensions, and in minor — three very different colours on the same architecture.
narrative.iiviInfoBox: Key principle: the tritone in V7 (B–F in G7) is the engine of resolution. B rises to C (3rd of CMaj7), F falls to E (7th of CMaj7) — an irresistible double chromatic movement that defines the jazz cadence.
narrative.iiviWarnBox: Essential practice: learn to play (or sing) the II–V–I in all 12 keys. Standards constantly modulate — knowing the progression in every key is the sine qua non for improvising freely.
narrative.rythmeIntro: Harmonic rhythm is the speed at which chords change. It is a fundamental dimension of a piece's identity — as important as the tempo or melody. Turnarounds are the end-of-chorus progressions that "turn" back to the repeat.
narrative.rythmeGiantDesc: Coltrane (1960). Keys change every 1–2 bars following a cycle of major thirds (C–E–Ab). Extremely dense harmonic rhythm.
narrative.ryhmeSoWhatDesc: Miles Davis (1959). D Dorian for 16 bars, Eb Dorian for 8 bars. Very slow modal harmonic rhythm — the colour settles and stays.
narrative.ryhmeCycleDesc: Chaining II–Vs in keys descending by fifths is the foundation of many standards. Dm7–G7 (II–V in C), then Gm7–C7 (II–V in F), then Cm7–F7 (II–V in Bb)... You'll recognise Autumn Leaves, All The Things You Are, and dozens of others.
narrative.rythmeRhythmChangesBox: Rhythm Changes: the I Got Rhythm chord changes (Gershwin, 1930) are the basis of hundreds of bebop standards — Anthropology, Oleo, Cottontail. 32-bar AABA structure: A section = I–VI–II–V repeated, B bridge = secondary dominants.

cours21.title: Analysis of Modern and Contemporary Composers
cours21.subtitle: Debussy, Ravel, Stravinsky, Messiaen, Satie, Beatles, Radiohead, Morricone — from impressionism to rock, how the 20th century reinvented harmony.

quiz.perfect: Excellent — 20th-century musical languages hold no more secrets!
quiz.veryGood: Well done! A few more composers to consolidate.
quiz.keepGoing: Keep listening — modern harmony reveals itself through the ear!

narrative.composersHeading: 8 COMPOSERS — From Impressionism to Contemporary Rock
narrative.composersIntro: From Debussy breaking tonality through colour, to Radiohead fusing Messiaen with electronics — the 20th century multiplied harmonic languages. Click each card to explore the techniques, hear the progression and understand the reference work.
narrative.analyseHeading: TONAL · MODAL · BITONAL — Three ways of thinking about harmony
narrative.analyseIntro: Compare the progressions, listen to both versions and identify the style. The ear recognises before the brain — trust your first impression.
narrative.quizScoreMsg0: Perfect! The languages of the 20th century hold no more secrets.
narrative.quizScoreMsg1: Very good! A few composers to revisit.
narrative.quizScoreMsg2: Keep exploring — modern harmony reveals itself through listening.
narrative.maitreCardConcept: Chords as colours — freeing harmony from the dominant-tonic resolution
narrative.maitreCardAnecdote: Debussy was expelled from the Paris Conservatoire several times for playing 'incorrect' chords. His teacher noted: 'He does as he pleases.' That was exactly his intention.
narrative.maitreCardLesson: Debussy treats chords like colours on a canvas — a 9th chord need not resolve; it can float and create an atmosphere, like a brushstroke of impressionist paint.

cours20.title: Analysis of the Great Classical Composers
cours20.subtitle: Bach, Mozart, Beethoven, Schubert, Chopin, Liszt, Berlioz, Tchaikovsky, Rachmaninoff — the harmonic signatures that shaped Western music.

quiz.perfect: Excellent — the great masters hold no more secrets!
quiz.veryGood: Well done! A few more composers to consolidate.
quiz.keepGoing: Keep exploring — each listen reveals new harmonic details!

narrative.compositeursIntro: Each composer develops a recognisable harmonic language. Click on each card to discover their techniques, listen to their signature progression and understand their reference work.
narrative.analyseIntro: Listen to the progressions, compare the versions and identify each composer's style. Ear-training analysis is the key to stylistic recognition.
narrative.quizPerfect: Perfect! You have mastered stylistic analysis.
narrative.quizGood: Very good! A few composers to review.
narrative.maitreCardConcept: Modulations by third & nascent romantic colours
narrative.maitreCardAnecdote: Schubert composed over 600 lieder, several in a single day. He died at 31 without ever having conducted most of his major works in public.
narrative.maitreCardLesson: Modulation by third — sliding from C major to A-flat major without a transition — creates a unique colour, like a sudden change of lighting on the same scene.

cours19.title: Introduction to Orchestration
cours19.subtitle: The 4 instrument families, their ranges and roles — doublings, balance, registers and SATB distribution in the orchestra.

quiz.perfect: Excellent — orchestration holds no more secrets!
quiz.veryGood: Well done! A few instrument families still to consolidate.
quiz.keepGoing: Keep exploring the instrument families — the ear learns with practice!

narrative.instrIntro: The symphony orchestra is organized into 4 major families. Each family has its own acoustic characteristics, ranges, and typical roles. Knowing these characteristics is the starting point for any orchestral writing.
narrative.instrInfoBox: Golden rule: the lower you go, the more you must space the voices. A C2–E2 chord sounds muddy; C2–G2–C3 sounds clear. In the upper register, thirds and seconds are perfectly readable. This rule guides the distribution of all orchestral and piano voices.
narrative.ecritureIntro: Orchestration is the art of assigning musical voices to instruments according to their characteristics. Five principles guide the writing: doubling, dynamic balance, register management, SATB distribution, and specific constraints for piano and chamber music.
narrative.ecritureWarnBox: Classic pitfalls: (1) Brass too loud — they cover the strings and woodwinds. (2) Low register too tight — muddy sound. (3) Uncovered range — harmonic gap. (4) Piano doubled in unison with strings — the timbres don't blend. Ravel consistently avoids these pitfalls: every note, every instrument, in its rightful place.
narrative.maitreAnecdote: In 1922, Sergei Diaghilev asked Ravel to orchestrate Mussorgsky's Pictures at an Exhibition for the Ballets Russes. Ravel spent months choosing each instrument for each tableau. When a colleague asked how he decided, he replied: 'I invent nothing. I hear the colour first — the timbre of the right instrument — and then I write the note.'
narrative.maitreLesson: Orchestration is not decoration: it is an essential part of composition. Before writing for an instrument, ask yourself: in which register does it sound best? What is its natural colour? How does it interact with the others? The ideal instrument played in its ideal register produces a beauty no other can replicate.
narrative.famCordesDesc: The backbone of the orchestra. Strings play tutti (the whole ensemble) or as separate sections. Their homogeneity of timbre allows blends and progressive crescendos impossible with other families.
narrative.famCordesI3Role: Harmonic foundation, reinforcement of the bass
narrative.famBoisDesc: Woodwinds bring colour and individuality. Each instrument has such a distinct timbre that they are heard even when doubled by strings. They provide intermediate doublings, lyrical solos, and counterpoint.
narrative.famBoisI0Timbre: Airy, luminous, fast in the upper register
narrative.famBoisI2Role: Middle harmony, solos, very versatile role
narrative.famBoisI3Timbre: Low, dark, slightly comic in the upper register
narrative.famBoisI3Role: Bass of the woodwinds, counterpoint, low doubling
narrative.famCuivresDesc: Brass are the most powerful instruments in the orchestra. Used sparingly at soft dynamics, they blend into the texture; at forte, they dominate everything. Ravel and Brahms master the art of using them at full power without masking the strings.
narrative.famCuivresI0Role: Link between woodwinds and brass, middle harmony, lyrical solos
narrative.famCuivresI1Range: F#3 – C6  (sounds as written or a tone lower)
narrative.famCuivresI1Timbre: Brilliant, bright, fanfare, cutting in the upper register
narrative.famCuivresI2Timbre: Powerful, deep, majestic — only glissando possible
narrative.famCuivresI2Role: Low harmony, brass chorals, bass of the brass
narrative.famCuivresI3Role: Low foundation of the brass, harmonic bass
narrative.famPercussionsDesc: Percussion structures the rhythm, underlines climaxes, and adds colour. Pitched instruments (timpani, xylophone) play precise pitches; unpitched instruments (snare drum, cymbals) add timbre and colour.
narrative.famPercussionsI0Range: Pitched — playing range C2 to A3
narrative.famPercussionsI0Timbre: Solemn, rolling, thunderous depending on stroke
narrative.famPianoDesc: The piano is a complete harmonic instrument — it can play melody, harmony, and bass simultaneously. In the orchestra, it is often used as a soloist or a percussive colour. The guitar is more common in chamber ensembles and jazz.
narrative.famPianoI0Role: Autonomous or solo instrument, percussive colour in the orchestra
narrative.prDoubluresDesc: Doubling an instrument means giving it the same melodic or harmonic line — in unison or at the octave. Doubling reinforces the sound without changing its function.
narrative.prDoubluresRule: Strings + Woodwinds → soft blend, rounded sound. Strings + Brass → power and brilliance. Avoid doubling fortissimo brass with woodwinds: the brass will cover them.
narrative.prDoubluresExample: Beethoven 5th: the G–G–G–Eb motif is played by strings and woodwinds together from bar 1 — unison doubling for maximum impact.
narrative.prEquilibreDesc: Balance between families is one of the major challenges of orchestration. A fortissimo brass section can cover 30 violins playing forte. Dynamic proportions must be chosen with care.
narrative.prEquilibreRule: Brass fortissimo: woodwinds and strings disappear. For strings to be heard at forte, brass should play mezzo-forte. The piano in the orchestra is often in opposition or as a soloist, rarely doubling.
narrative.prEquilibreExample: Ravel's Boléro: the same melody and harmony are repeated 18 times with progressively denser orchestration — a model of cumulative balance.
narrative.prRegistresDesc: The orchestra covers a spectrum from C0 (bass/low tuba) to C8 (piccolo/high violin). The orchestrator's role is to fill this spectrum harmonically without creating 'gaps' or 'overcrowding'.
narrative.prRegistresRule: Avoid gaps: if the tenor jumps to soprano without a middle voice, the sound seems hollow. Avoid overcrowding: too many notes in the same register creates muddy sound. Space the voices: the lower the voices, the more they must be spaced.
