narrative.prRegistresExample: Fundamental rule: low intervals should be wide (fifths, octaves) and high ones can be narrow (thirds, seconds). A C major triad: in the bass = C2–G2–C3; in the treble = C4–E4–G4.
narrative.prSatbDesc: The distribution of counterpoint voices (Soprano/Alto/Tenor/Bass) maps naturally onto orchestral families according to their respective ranges.
narrative.prSatbRule: Soprano (S) → Violin I, Flute, Oboe, Trumpet. Alto (A) → Violin II, Viola, Clarinet, Horn. Tenor (T) → Cello, Trombone, Bassoon. Bass (B) → Double bass, Tuba, low Bassoon.
narrative.prSatbExample: A Bach chorale transcribed for orchestra: bass in cellos/double basses, tenor in violas/low horns, alto in violins II/clarinets, soprano in violins I/oboes.
narrative.prPianoDesc: Pianistic writing follows specific constraints related to the two hands, the sustain pedal, and the instrument's ideal range.
narrative.prPianoRule: Right hand: melody + upper harmony (register C4–C6). Left hand: bass + accompaniment (register A0–C4). Sustain pedal: prolongs and connects harmonies. Avoid too many notes in the low register (muddy sound) and too many in the high register (shrill).
narrative.prPianoExample: Chopin Nocturne: lyrical melody in the right hand (register E4–G5), wide arpeggios in the left hand (register C2–G3), sustain pedal held throughout the bar.
narrative.exRavelDesc: The same snare-drum rhythmic ostinato, the same melody in C major, the same harmony — repeated 18 times with progressively denser orchestration. Each repetition adds instruments or changes combinations. A real-time study in orchestration.
narrative.exDebussyName: Debussy — Prélude à l'après-midi d'un faune (1894)
narrative.exDebussyDesc: Orchestral impressionism at its finest: solo flute, harp, muted strings, stopped horns, woodwinds in half-tints. Each instrument in its most poetic register. No loud brass — everything is suggestion and colour.
narrative.exBeethovenDesc: A model of economy of means: a 4-note motif distributed across all families. The orchestration follows the intensity of the discourse — strings + woodwinds at p, tutti fortissimo at key moments. Every instrument in its rightful place.

cours18.title: Motivic Development
cours18.subtitle: The 5 elements of a motif, the repetition paradox, and the 4 families of techniques — from harmony (least radical) to rhythm (most radical).

quiz.perfect: Excellent — motivic development holds no more secrets!
quiz.veryGood: Well done! A few more techniques to consolidate.
quiz.keepGoing: Keep listening to the transformations — the ear learns with practice!

narrative.maitreCardAnecdote: In 1808, Beethoven premiered his 5th Symphony. The opening motif — G–G–G–E♭, just four notes — generates twenty minutes of music. A student asked how he could build so much from so little. Beethoven replied: “That is exactly the right question. Richness lies not in the quantity of material — it lies in the depth with which you develop it.”
narrative.maitreCardLesson: Before composing, ask yourself: is my opening motif short enough to be memorable? Distinctive enough to be recognisable? Flexible enough to be transformed? A motif that answers yes to all three can generate a symphony. One that is too complex is hard to develop; too simple, it exhausts itself quickly.
narrative.motifP1: The motif is the smallest distinct and immediately recognisable musical unit. It serves as the guiding thread of musical discourse: repeated and transformed, it gives coherence and direction to a phrase, a movement, or an entire work. Unlike support music (which accompanies an external action), discourse music develops its own ideas autonomously.
narrative.motifDemoTitle: The most famous motif: Beethoven's 5th Symphony
narrative.motifDemoDesc: Just 4 notes — 3 repeated + a descending minor third. This motif is fragmented, transposed, augmented and liquidated across all 4 movements of the symphony: 20 minutes of music from a 4-note cell.
narrative.motifH3Elements: The 5 constituent elements of a motif
narrative.elem1Desc: The characteristic melodic leaps — the distinctive melodic identity (third, fifth, tritone). Beethoven's motif: a descending minor third G→E♭.
narrative.elem2Desc: The distinctive rhythmic pattern — often more identifiable than the melody. Three short notes + one long (ta-ta-ta-TAA) in Beethoven.
narrative.elem3Desc: The chords that support and colour the motif — determining whether the expression feels tense, resolved, or ambiguous.
narrative.elem4Desc: The texture surrounding the motif: arpeggios, block chords, independent bass line, counterpoint.
narrative.elem5Desc: Nuance, articulation, attack — the physical expressive character. Forte or piano, legato or staccato.
narrative.motifWarnBox: The paradox of repetition: repetition is necessary — without it, the listener recognises nothing and the musical discourse loses all coherence. But it is dangerous if too pure: repeating identically more than twice quickly breeds monotony. The solution is to repeat while transforming — maintain recognition while renewing interest.
narrative.motifH3Rhetorique: The motif as character: 3 rhetorical stages
narrative.step1Desc: Establish the motif's identity — present it clearly and unambiguously.
narrative.step2Desc: Anchor the motif in the listener's ear — repeat (transformed) to confirm the identity.
narrative.step3Desc: Transform, develop, combine — the motif travels and matures.
narrative.ex1Desc: Just four notes. Beethoven fragments, transposes, augments and liquidates this motif throughout all four movements. Twenty minutes of symphony from a four-note cell.
narrative.ex2Motif: Motif associated with a character or idea
narrative.ex2Desc: Wagner assigns a motif to each character (Siegfried's sword, the curse of the Gold). The motif returns transformed according to the dramatic state — a motif of glory becomes sinister through harmonic change.
narrative.ex3Desc: Williams inherits directly from Wagner: each character or concept has its leitmotif. The Force theme is transformed into a military march for Darth Vader — same contour, radically different harmonic context.
narrative.motifInfoBox: The motif is not an ornament — it is the seed of all musical thought. Before composing a phrase, ask yourself: is my core idea distinctive enough to be recognisable? Flexible enough to be transformed in 10 different ways? A good idea generates a phrase; an excellent idea generates a symphony.
narrative.techP1: Development techniques are organised into 4 families, ranked from least to most radical. We begin by transforming the context (harmony, dynamics) before touching the melody, and we modify rhythm last — as it is often the most identifying element of the motif.
narrative.famAOrder: First — the least radical changes. The melodic and rhythmic identity of the motif remains intact.
narrative.famADesc: Transforming the harmonic context or the accompanying texture. The motif remains fully recognisable because its melody and rhythm are preserved — only the 'colour' changes.
narrative.famASub1Detail: Replace I with VI (diatonic substitution), or V7 with its tritone substitute. Same melody, different harmonic colour.
narrative.famASub2Detail: Transform the bass into independent counterpoint — parallel thirds or sixths, or contrary motion to the melody.
narrative.famASub3Detail: Break chords into successive ascending or descending notes. Transforms the texture without touching the melodic motif.
narrative.famASub4Detail: Add an independent voice that answers or imitates the motif. Enriches polyphonic density.
narrative.famBOrder: Second — expressive changes without altering notes or rhythm.
narrative.famBDesc: Modifying expressive parameters: dynamics, articulation, register, timbre. The motif keeps its structure intact — only its 'energy' or 'costume' changes.
narrative.famBSub1Detail: Forte → piano, crescendo, decrescendo. Same melody, radically different dramatic intensity contrast.
narrative.famBSub2Detail: Legato → staccato, accents on unexpected beats. Changes the character without touching pitches or durations.
narrative.famBSub3Detail: Transpose by an octave: going up brings brightness and urgency; going down creates gravity and depth.
narrative.famBSub4Detail: Change the instrument or register: same motif in the strings, then brass, then tutti — entirely different effects.
narrative.famCOrder: Third — the pitches change, but the rhythm often remains recognisable.
narrative.famCDesc: Modifying the melodic contour of the motif while often maintaining its rhythmic structure. The ear recognises the motif by its rhythm even when pitches change significantly.
narrative.famCSub1Detail: Each interval is inverted: an ascending third becomes a descending third. G–G–G–E♭ becomes G–G–G–B♭.
narrative.famCSub2Detail: The motif is played backwards — from the last note to the first. E♭–G–G–G (retrograde of Beethoven's motif).
narrative.famCSub3Detail: Insert passing notes, or remove a note from the motif. The contour remains recognisable, lightened or enriched.
narrative.famCSub4Detail: Pitches shift according to the scale (harmonic sequence) but the rhythmic pattern remains identical.
narrative.famDOrder: Last — the most radical changes. The motif may become unrecognisable.
narrative.famDDesc: Modifying note durations or metric structure. Rhythmic alterations are the most transformative: rhythm is often the most distinctive feature of a motif — to touch it is to risk it.
narrative.famDSub1Detail: Double (or triple) the duration of each note. The motif slows down, takes on a solemn grandeur — Beethoven uses this in sonata developments.
narrative.famDSub2Detail: Divide the duration of each note. The motif speeds up, becomes nervous or urgent — liquidation effect at the end of a phrase.
narrative.famDSub3Detail: Moving from 4/4 to 3/4 (or vice versa). The same motif in a different metre creates a feeling of imbalance or lightness.
narrative.famDSub4Detail: Transform syncopations into strong beats (regularisation) or strong beats into syncopations (deregulation). Changes rhythmic energy without touching pitches.
narrative.techArpegeFormula: C – E – G – C  (ascending broken chord)
narrative.techArpegeDesc: Accompanying with arpeggios transforms the texture without touching the melodic motif.
narrative.techContrepointDesc: Two voices in contrary motion — the bass ascends while the upper voice descends.
narrative.techTipBox: Combinations: the 4 families combine freely. A motif can simultaneously receive a harmonic substitution (A), be played pianissimo (B), and be slightly transposed (C). Combinations create the richest transformations — this is exactly what Beethoven does in his great developments.

cours17.title: Musical Phrase and Form
cours17.subtitle: Motif, 4-step development, repetition techniques, antecedent-consequent period and major musical forms.

quiz.perfect: Excellent — musical phrase and form hold no more secrets!
quiz.veryGood: Well done! A few forms still to consolidate.
quiz.keepGoing: Keep listening to the examples — the ear learns to recognise forms with practice!

narrative.phraseIntro: The musical phrase is the basic unit of musical discourse. It rests on a motif — the smallest distinctive musical element — which is repeated and transformed in 4 typical steps. Unlike support music (which accompanies an action), discourse music develops its own ideas autonomously.
narrative.beethovenBoxDesc: Just 4 notes — and yet the seed of an entire symphony. This motif is fragmented, transposed, augmented, and liquidated throughout all 4 movements.
narrative.stepsTitle: The 4 steps of a phrase (8 bars)
narrative.step1Desc: Presentation of the main motif — the statement of the musical subject.
narrative.step2Desc: Strict or transformed — anchors the motif in the listener's ear.
narrative.step3Desc: Fragmentation and liquidation — the harmonic rhythm accelerates.
narrative.step4Desc: Conclusive harmonic resolution (V for half-cadence, I for perfect cadence).
narrative.liquidationBox: Liquidation (Schoenberg's term): at the end of a phrase (step 3), the motif progressively loses its distinctive characteristics. First the rhythm is retained, then just the contour, then almost nothing — the phrase dissolves into the cadence. A highly expressive rhetorical device for 'conclusion'.
narrative.techStricteDesc: The motif is repeated identically — same pitches, same rhythm. The most direct technique: the listener recognizes the motif unambiguously. Used only once before varying.
narrative.techTransfoDesc: The motif is transposed to another pitch while retaining its rhythmic contour. The rhythmic identity maintains coherence; the new pitch brings variety.
narrative.techMarcheDesc: The motif repeats at successive scale degrees (tonal sequence) or by strict transposition (real sequence). Schoenberg carefully distinguishes the two types: the tonal sequence stays diatonic, the real sequence may leave the scale.
narrative.techComplementaireDesc: A second motif answers the first and completes the rhythmic or melodic space. Together, the two motifs form the complete phrase — each is incomplete without the other.
narrative.techHarmoniqueDesc: The melodic contour is preserved but the chords underneath change, altering the harmonic colour. The melody is recognizable; the harmony renews the expression.
narrative.warnBox: Key point: repetition must be varied — repeating identically more than twice creates monotony. The art is to maintain recognition of the motif (coherence) while renewing it (variety). Beethoven never repeats the same motif in exactly the same way twice in a row.
narrative.periodeTitle: The period and the large forms
narrative.periodeIntro: The period is a structure of two phrases: the antecedent asks a question (weak cadence on V), the consequent provides the answer (strong cadence on I). The large forms organize these periods into contrasting sections to build a musical discourse on a larger scale.
narrative.antecedentDesc: The half-cadence on V leaves the phrase 'open' — the ear awaits a resolution.
narrative.consequentDesc: The perfect cadence on I resolves the tension — the 'answer' is given, the period is complete.
narrative.periodCompleteBtn: ▶ Listen to the complete period (antecedent + consequent)
narrative.formeAbaDesc: Section A (main), contrasting section B (new key or mode), return of A. The symmetry creates balance and satisfaction: we depart, we return.
narrative.formeAbaExamples: Chopin Nocturnes, opera arias (aria da capo)
narrative.formeAABADesc: Section A (8 bars) repeated twice, contrasting bridge B (8 bars), return of A (8 bars). Standard form of American jazz from the 1930s–60s. The bridge B is often in a neighbouring key.
narrative.formeAABAExamples: Autumn Leaves, I Got Rhythm, Stardust, Over the Rainbow
narrative.formeBluesDesc: Question/answer/resolution in 12 bars: tonic statement (4 bars), subdominant response (2 bars), return (2 bars), turnaround (4 bars). Predictable structure exploited by musicians and improvisers.
narrative.formeBluesExamples: 12-bar blues, jazz blues with II–V substitutions
narrative.formeABACDesc: Variant of the standard form: A returns twice but ends differently (B then C). Each return of A creates a slight surprise in the conclusion.
narrative.formeABACExamples: All The Things You Are, My Romance
narrative.formeSonateDesc: Exposition: theme A (tonic) + theme B (dominant/relative). Development: thematic manipulation in distant keys. Recapitulation: A and B in tonic. The most complex form of classicism.
narrative.formeSonateExamples: 1st movement of sonatas by Beethoven, Mozart, Haydn
narrative.tipBox: Jazz and form: an improviser who knows the form plays 'with' the structure. More tension in bridge B, more marked resolution at the return of A, climax at the right moment of the chorus. Ignoring the form means improvising in a vacuum — technically correct, musically inexpressive. Form is the framework of discourse.
narrative.maitreCardAnecdote: At UCLA in the 1940s, Schoenberg asked his students to analyze Beethoven's 5th Symphony: 'See how these four notes — Sol–Sol–Sol–Mib — generate twenty minutes of symphony. A motif is not an ornament: it is the seed of all musical thought.' His treatise Fundamentals of Musical Composition formalises these principles for his American classes.
narrative.maitreCardLesson: The motif is the smallest unit of musical discourse. Before composing a phrase, ask yourself: is my basic idea distinctive enough to be recognizable? Flexible enough to be transformed? A good idea generates a phrase; an excellent idea generates a symphony.
narrative.beethovenFormulaCaption: Sol – Sol – Sol – Mib  (3 repetitions + minor third drop)

cours11.title: Chord Extensions
cours11.subtitle: 9ths, 11ths, 13ths — stacking thirds beyond the 7th to enrich harmonic color.

quiz.perfect: Excellent — chord extensions hold no more secrets!
quiz.veryGood: Well done! A few more chords to practice and you'll have it.
quiz.keepGoing: Keep listening actively — the ear learns with practice!

narrative.extIntro: A basic chord (triad or tetrad) can be enriched by extensions: we continue stacking thirds beyond the 7th. The 9th is the 2nd an octave higher, the 11th is the 4th an octave higher, the 13th is the 6th an octave higher.
narrative.extInfoBox: The chain of thirds: root → 3rd → 5th → 7th → 9th → 11th → 13th. Each step adds a third. A complete 13th chord contains 7 notes — the entire scale at once. In practice, several are omitted to maintain clarity.
narrative.extClickHint: Click an extension to hear it and see its details.
narrative.maitreAnecdote: Bill Evans had studied Ravel, Debussy, and Chopin before joining Miles Davis in 1958. When Kind of Blue was recorded, Evans introduced a radically new voicing style: 9ths in inner voices, 11ths in clusters, 13ths replacing 5ths. His trio (with Scott LaFaro and Paul Motian) redefined chamber jazz. Waltz for Debby (1961) remains the benchmark for piano extensions.
narrative.maitreLesson: A 9th doesn't 'replace' a chord tone — it enriches it. The difference between a chord and a color is often just one note an octave higher.
narrative.appIntro: Extensions apply to all three tonal functions (T, SD, D) without changing their role in the progression. Dm9 remains subdominant, G13 remains dominant, CMaj9 remains tonic — but each chord gains richness and color.
narrative.appTensionsInfoBox: The available tensions rule: an extension is « available » if it does not create a ♭9 interval (semitone + octave) with a chord tone. The ♭9 is the unacceptable dissonance — except when it is itself the desired tension.
narrative.appWarnBox: Key exception: the perfect 11th is avoided on major chords (it creates a ♭9 with the major 3rd), but it is available and very common on minor chords (the ♭3 is a whole tone away from the 11th). That is why Dm11 works, but CMaj11 sounds wrong.
narrative.appTipBox: Practical voicing: in jazz piano, keep 3rd + 7th as the core (shell voicing) and add the desired tension in the soprano or an inner voice. The root is played by the bass — no need to double it at the piano.
narrative.quizFeedbackExcellent: Excellent — chord extensions hold no more secrets!
narrative.quizFeedbackGood: Good — a few more chords to practice and you'll be perfect.
narrative.quizFeedbackKeepGoing: Keep listening to extensions actively — the ear learns!

cours22.title: Reharmonization
cours22.subtitle: Transform a progression while preserving the melody — diatonic and tritone substitutions, modal borrowing, parallel harmonization.

quiz.perfect: Excellent — reharmonization holds no more secrets!
quiz.veryGood: Well done! A few more progressions to consolidate.
quiz.keepGoing: Keep listening to the progressions — the ear learns by doing!

narrative.principeIntro: Reharmonising means replacing the chords of a progression while keeping the melody intact. The fundamental constraint: every melody note must remain consonant with the new chord.
narrative.principeInfoBox: Principle: the melody is fixed — it is "the roof". The chords can change — they are "the floor". Choosing a new chord means choosing the light under which the melody will be heard.
narrative.principeClickHint: Click on a technique to see and hear the progressions.
narrative.principeWarnBox: Melodic check: before any reharmonisation, identify the long notes of the melody. A held note that creates a half-step with a chord tone = strong dissonance. Check systematically.
narrative.applicationIntro: Three examples from the jazz repertoire, each illustrating a different technique. Compare the original and reharmonised progressions by listening to them.
narrative.applicationTip: Exercise: take a standard you know (Autumn Leaves, All The Things You Are) and reharmonise 2–3 chords using the techniques from this lesson. Start with diatonic substitution — it is the closest to the original.
narrative.quizFeedbackExcellent: Excellent — reharmonisation holds no more secrets!
narrative.quizFeedbackGood: Good — just a few more progressions to listen to and it will be perfect.
narrative.quizFeedbackKeepGoing: Keep listening to progressions actively — the ear learns!
narrative.maitreAnecdote: In 1958, Bill Evans recorded 'Peace Piece' in a single take. For eight minutes, he explored every harmonic colour possible over a single bass pedal — C. He borrowed chords from the minor mode, slid chromatic Maj7 blocks down, never leaving the root C. This piece is a manifesto of reharmonisation as colour.
narrative.maitreLesson: Reharmonising means seeing the same notes under different lights. The melody is the soloist — the chords are the light you choose to illuminate it.

cours23.title: Composing in the style of the masters
cours23.subtitle: Bach, Mozart, Chopin, Debussy, Jazz, Rock — learn to identify and reproduce the harmonic signatures that define each great style.

quiz.perfect: Perfect! You have mastered the great stylistic signatures.
quiz.veryGood: Very good! A few nuances to refine between close styles.
quiz.keepGoing: Keep exploring the styles — restart when you are ready.

narrative.maitreCardAnecdote: Brahms spent years studying Bach and Beethoven before publishing his first symphony at age 43. He said he could hear the footsteps of giants behind him. Yet his style is immediately recognizable: dense harmonies, syncopated rhythms, Nordic melancholy. Studying the masters does not dilute one's style — it forges it.
narrative.maitreCardLesson: Knowing the styles of others is the shortest path to finding your own.
narrative.stylesIntro: Every composer or movement has a harmonic signature — a set of techniques, progressions and colors that make it immediately recognizable. Click on a style to hear its typical progression.
narrative.stylesInfoBox: How to recognize a style? Look for these 3 clues: (1) which chords are used (diatonic? borrowed? extensions?); (2) how the chords follow each other (by fifths? by thirds? in parallel?); (3) is there a V7→I resolution or not?
narrative.stylesClickHint: Click on a style to see its characteristics and hear its typical progression.
narrative.stylesWarnBox: Styles are not rigid boxes: a composer can blend several styles in a single piece. Chopin borrows from Bach (counterpoint) and anticipates Debussy (modal borrowings). Recognizing a style means identifying the dominant language, not making an absolute classification.
narrative.exIntro: For each style, choose the progression that matches it. Listen to each option before answering — the ear guides theory.
narrative.exTipBox: Method: listen to all options first (▶ button) before choosing. The ear recognizes the style before the theory — trust your listening, then check with the explanations.
narrative.quizScoreGood: Excellent — the masters' styles hold no more secrets!
narrative.quizScoreOk: Good — a few more comparative listens and you'll have it.
narrative.quizScoreLow: Keep listening to the progressions of each style — the ear learns!

cours24.title: Augmented Sixth Chords
cours24.subtitle: It+6, Fr+6, Ger+6 — three Romantic altered chords, their contrary-motion resolution, and their use in 19th-century music.

quiz.perfect: Perfect! You have mastered all three augmented sixths and their resolutions.
quiz.veryGood: Very good! A few details to refine on resolutions and usage.
quiz.keepGoing: Keep studying augmented sixth chords — restart when you are ready.

narrative.maitreCardAnecdote: Liszt used the German augmented sixth as his harmonic signature — a chord that concentrates all romantic tension in 3 or 4 notes.
narrative.maitreCardLesson: One well-placed altered chord is worth ten ordinary ones.
narrative.pTypes: The augmented sixth chord is an altered pre-dominant chord characterized by the augmented sixth interval between bVI (in the bass) and #IV. Three variants exist — Italian, French and German — each with its own construction and color.
narrative.infoBoxContext: Context in C major: In all the examples below, we are in C major. bVI = Ab, I = C, #IV = F#. These three notes form the common core of all augmented sixth chords.
narrative.clickHint: Click a chord to expand it and listen.
narrative.infoBoxRegle: Universal rule of augmented sixths: Regardless of type (It+6, Fr+6, Al+6), the augmented sixth interval (bVI–#IV) always resolves by contrary motion to the dominant fifth. In C major: Ab moves down to G, F# moves up to G. This convergence is mandatory.
narrative.pApplication: Augmented sixth chords do not appear randomly — they follow a precise harmonic scheme and reveal their full power in late Baroque, Classical and Romantic music.
narrative.infoBoxSchema:
Classic scheme: IVm → It+6/Fr+6/Al+6 → V → I
The augmented sixth is a pre-dominant chord: it always appears before V (or I6/4–V). The minor subdominant (IVm) prepares it gently.
narrative.infoBoxJazz: Al+6 and the tritone substitution: The German sixth Ab–C–Eb–F# is enharmonically identical to Ab7 (Ab–C–Eb–G♭). In jazz in C major, Ab7 = bII7 (written as 'Db7'). Jazz musicians exploit this ambiguity for tritone substitution: replacing G7 (V7) with Db7 (bII7) works for exactly the same reason that Al+6 creates tension toward V.
narrative.warnBox: Common pitfall: Do not confuse Al+6 with V7/IV (same chord, opposite functions). Al+6 resolves to V (Ab and F# converge to G by contrary motion). V7/IV (Ab7) resolves to IV = Db major (normal dominant resolution). The same combination of notes can lead in two completely different directions depending on context.
narrative.sixteItDesc: The simplest of the three — only 3 voices. The augmented sixth in its purest form.
narrative.sixtefrDesc: Contains the dominant ninth (D). More colorful than the Italian, it already hints at the sound of V9.
narrative.sixteAlDesc: The richest and most commonly used. Enharmonically identical to a dominant seventh chord (Ab7), but its resolution is different.
narrative.sixteAlResolution: Resolves to V with doubled fifth (to avoid parallel fifths)
narrative.epoqueBaroque: Bach and Handel use the augmented sixth at important cadences. The chord is functional, without expressive excess — purely structural.
narrative.epoqueClassique: Mozart and Beethoven codify the formula Al+6 → I6/4 → V → I as the par excellence final cadence. The augmented sixth acquires its rhetorical role.
narrative.epoqueRomantique: Schubert, Schumann, Brahms and Liszt use it expressionistically: modulations via enharmony, unexpected progressions, dramatic delays.
narrative.epoqueJazz: Jazz rediscovers the equivalence Al+6 ↔ bII7 as tritone substitution. Db7 in C = enharmonic Ab7 = functional Al+6 — same tension, different language.
narrative.commentSchubert: Schubert uses Al+6 to create a moment of intense melancholy before the resolution. The harmonic richness of the chord amplifies the dark color of the lied.
narrative.commentBeethoven: Beethoven uses the Italian sixth (3 voices, absolute purity) for a targeted dramatic effect. The chord's restraint reinforces the moment's intensity.
narrative.commentBrahms: Brahms exploits Fr+6 for its particular color: D (2nd degree = dominant 9th) prefigures the sound of V9 and enriches the final cadence.
narrative.bassePremierRenversement: Always in first inversion — bass on bVI:
narrative.bassePremierRenversementDesc: Ab (bVI) is invariably in the bass → it descends by semitone to G (dominant fifth)

cours25.title: Chromaticism and Advanced Chromatic Harmony
cours25.subtitle: Chromatic lines, the Tristan chord, enharmonic modulation — exploring the limits of tonality with Wagner and Liszt.

quiz.perfect: Perfect! You have mastered chromaticism and tonal ambiguity.
quiz.veryGood: Very good! A few nuances to refine on enharmony and resolutions.
quiz.keepGoing: Keep exploring chromaticism — restart when you are ready.

narrative.maitreCardAnecdote: The Prelude to Tristan und Isolde opens with a chord that theorists still debate today. Wagner pushed chromaticism so far that tonality itself becomes uncertain — opening the path to Schoenberg and 20th-century music.
narrative.maitreCardLesson: Chromaticism is not decoration — it is a deeper harmonic logic.
narrative.subtitle: Chromatic lines, Tristan chord, enharmonic modulation — tonality pushed to its limits.
narrative.pLignes: Chromaticism is organized into directional lines — movements by semitones that create their own logic, distinct but complementary to diatonic harmony. Each chromatic line carries a precise expressive meaning.
narrative.pClickHint: Click a line to explore it and listen.
narrative.infoBoxRegle:
Universal rule of chromatic resolution:
♯ rises (C# → D) · ♭ descends (Db → C)
Never resolve a chromatic note in the direction opposite to its accidental — this is the most common mistake in harmonization.
narrative.descAscendant: Leads upward. Expresses aspiration, dramatic rise, desire.
narrative.descDescendant: Darkens downward. Expresses resignation, lamentation, mourning — the chromaticism of the Baroque lamento.
narrative.pAmbiguite: Chromaticism pushed to the extreme creates chords whose tonal function becomes deliberately ambiguous. Wagner, Liszt and their contemporaries explored this borderland where tonality itself wavers.
narrative.tristanIntro: This chord opens the Prelude of Tristan und Isolde (1865). For 160 years, theorists have debated its analysis:
narrative.tristanAnalyse2exp: G#7 (= Ab7) in inversion, 2nd inversion with added 9th
narrative.tristanAnalyse3exp: Altered VII9 of A minor — G# is drawn toward A
narrative.tristanConclusion: Conclusion: The ambiguity IS the message. Wagner refuses any clear resolution — the key of A minor is suggested but never confirmed, creating permanent tension throughout the prelude (16 minutes).
narrative.dim7Intro: The diminished 7th is symmetrical — built from equal minor thirds. This makes it enharmonically ambiguous: the same chord can be reinterpreted in 4 different ways, resolving to 4 distinct keys.
narrative.dim7Conclusion: Enharmonic modulation: Changing the interpretation of a chord without changing its notes. The same sound, a new tonal direction — a fundamental technique of Schubert, Liszt and Wagner for imperceptible modulations.
narrative.warnBox:
Common pitfalls:
· Gratuitous chromaticism without direction — every altered note must have a logical resolution
· Enharmonic confusion: G# (rises → A) ≠ Ab (descends → G)
· Losing the tonal center by accumulating chromaticisms without diatonic anchoring
narrative.lamentoDesc: Chromatic descent of a fourth, from tonic to dominant. The most expressive bass line of the Baroque — it evokes mourning, lamentation, the inevitable. Purcell, Handel, Pachelbel all use it for their most intense moments.
narrative.lamentoExemple: C – B – Bb – A – Ab – G (in C minor)
narrative.sensibilisationDesc: Chromatic ascent by semitones — each note is sensitized toward the next. Creates growing, inevitable tension. Common in Romanticism to express aspiration, desire, dramatic rise.
narrative.sensibilisationExemple: C – C# – D – D# – E – F (in C major)
narrative.broderieDesc: Auxiliary note a semitone from the main note — tighter than a diatonic neighbor. The effect is of intensified expression, a shiver around a central note. Bach and Chopin use it with great refinement.

cours30.title: Impressionist Harmony and Advanced Modality
cours30.subtitle: Planing, whole-tone scale, octatonic, pentatonic and Messiaen modes — harmonic color as an autonomous musical language.

quiz.perfect: Perfect! You have mastered impressionist harmony techniques.
quiz.veryGood: Very good! A few nuances to refine on special scales and planing.
quiz.keepGoing: Keep exploring impressionism — restart when you are ready.

narrative.maitreCardAnecdote: Ravel said he was not trying to express emotions but sound colours. His Boléro — a single C major chord for 15 minutes — proves that colour can replace function. Debussy called him 'the clockmaker of sound'.
narrative.maitreCardLesson: When the chord becomes colour, tension is no longer functional — it is sensory.
narrative.pImpressionnisme: Impressionist harmony breaks with the tension-resolution logic of the tonal system. Debussy and Ravel replace harmonic function with sound colour — each chord is a shade, not a role.
narrative.infoBoxPlaning: Planing: moving an entire chord in parallel motion. Parallel fifths and octaves are intentional — they create the effect of a sliding sound block. Do not analyse with T/SD/D functions.
narrative.gamme0description: No leading tone — tonal floating is total. Debussy is its master. All chords are augmented.
narrative.gamme1description: Alternating tone/semitone. Contains 3 diminished 7th chords. Stravinsky and Messiaen loved it.
narrative.gamme2description: 5 notes without any semitone, hence no leading tension. Used in jazz, folk and oriental impressionism.
narrative.gamme3description: Chord of 3 equal semitones. Symmetric — can resolve to 3 different keys. Characteristic of impressionist harmony.
narrative.pPlaning: Three successive Maj7 chords chromatically transposed a tone upward — no resolution, pure sliding colour.
narrative.warnBoxImpressionnisme: Common mistake: Analysing Debussy's harmony with T/SD/D functions. Impressionist chords have no functional role — looking for a dominant in 'Voiles' is looking in the wrong place. Analyse by colour and register.
narrative.pModalite: Messiaen theorised modes of limited transposition — scales whose internal symmetry reduces the number of distinct possible transpositions. Each mode has a unique and recognisable colour.
narrative.infoBoxTranspositions: Limited transpositions: A mode of limited transposition is a scale whose internal symmetry causes certain transpositions to reproduce the same collection of notes. Mode 1: 2 transpositions. Mode 2: 3 transpositions. The lower the number, the stronger the symmetry.
narrative.mode2exemple: Messiaen, 'Quartet for the End of Time'
narrative.h3ModalInterchange: Modal interchange (borrowed chords in C major)
narrative.pModalInterchange: Modal borrowing consists of temporarily using a chord from a parallel mode (same tonic, different mode) without actually modulating. C remains the tonal centre.
narrative.mi0effet: Sudden dark colour — the borrowed IVm is the most expressive
narrative.mi2effet: Romantic colour — very expressive borrowed chord
narrative.warnBoxModalInterchange: Modal interchange ≠ modulation: Modal borrowing is temporary — the tonic does not change. In C major, borrowing Ab (bVI) creates a colour, but C remains the centre. A modulation implies a change of tonal centre confirmed by a cadence.
narrative.h2Quiz: Quiz — Impressionist harmony and advanced modality

cours31.title: Polytonality and Quartal Harmony
cours31.subtitle: Superimposed tonalities (Stravinsky, Pétrouchka), quartal chords, the So What chord — musical languages beyond classical tonality.

quiz.perfect: Perfect! You have mastered polytonality and quartal harmony.
quiz.veryGood: Very good! A few points to consolidate on quartal voicings.
quiz.keepGoing: Keep exploring polytonality — restart when you are ready.

narrative.maitreCardAnecdote: The premiere of 'The Rite of Spring' in 1913 provoked a riot in the hall. Stravinsky superimposed tonalities so distant that the audience thought it was a mistake. The Petrushka chord — C major against F# major, a tritone — is the signature of this chosen brutality.
narrative.maitreCardLesson: Polytonal tension is not a mistake — it is a language. The distance between tonalities controls the intensity of the clash.
narrative.pPolytonalite: Polytonality superimposes two (or more) distinct tonalities at the same time. Unlike atonality, each layer remains tonal — it is their combination that creates tension or ambiguity.
narrative.infoBoxBitonalite: Bitonality vs polytonality: Bitonality is the most common form — exactly two superimposed tonalities. The distance between the tonics determines the intensity of the clash: third (gentle) → fifth (blurry) → tritone (brutal). Stravinsky favoured the tritone for its maximum tension.
narrative.ex0description: The famous 'Petrushka chord' superimposes C major (C–E–G) and F# major (F#–A#–C#). Interval between the tonics: a tritone — the maximum tension.
narrative.ex0couleur: Brutal tension, tonal clash, tragedy of the character
narrative.ex1description: Two tonalities a fifth apart: much less tension than a tritone. Natural harmonics make C and G close — the polytonality is subtle.
narrative.ex2description: G major chord over F# bass — the F# creates tension with the root G. Common in progressive rock and jazz fusion.
narrative.warnBoxPolytonalite: Polytonality ≠ atonality: In polytonal music, tonal centres are still audible — simply several at once. Analysis must identify each layer separately. Confusing the two systems is the most frequent mistake.
narrative.infoBoxAnalysePolytonale: Polytonal analysis: First identify each layer independently, then note the distance between the tonics (third, fifth, tritone...). The effect depends directly on this distance: tonics a third apart = gentle polytonality (Milhaud); tonics a tritone apart = maximum clash (Stravinsky).
narrative.pQuartale: Quartal harmony replaces the stacking of thirds (C–E–G) with the stacking of fourths (C–F–Bb). Without a third, the chord is neither major nor minor — it creates the modern ambiguity characteristic of modal jazz and 20th-century music.
narrative.infoBoxQuartale: Why fourths change everything: A chord in thirds (triad) has a defined quality (major/minor) and a strong tonal function. A chord in fourths has neither clear quality nor function — it floats. It is precisely this neutrality that Miles Davis and McCoy Tyner exploited in modal jazz.
narrative.aq0description: Three stacked perfect fourths: C–F–Bb. No third → neither major nor minor.
narrative.aq0effet: Pure ambiguity — the chord does not 'pull' toward a resolution
narrative.aq1description: Perfect fourth + augmented fourth (tritone): C–F–B. The final tritone creates a slight harshness.
narrative.aq1structure: C + perfect fourth → F + tritone → B
narrative.aq1effet: More tense than the pure fourth — characteristic harshness
narrative.aq2description: 4-note voicing in fourths: D–G–C–F. Signature of the modal jazz piano (Coltrane quartet).
narrative.aq2effet: Immediately recognisable 'modal jazz' sound — total harmonic openness
narrative.aq3description: Miles Davis's signature chord from 'So What': 4 fourths + major third at the top. D–G–C–F–A.
narrative.aq3effet: Emblematic modal jazz chord — spacious, neither major nor minor
narrative.h3SoWhat: Building the So What chord on any note
narrative.pSoWhat: The So What chord always follows the same formula: Note + fourth + fourth + fourth + major third. On D: D–G–C–F–A. This formula transposes exactly everywhere.
narrative.warnBoxQuartale: Common mistake: Analysing quartal chords with T/SD/D functions. Quartal harmony works by colour and ambiguity, not by functional progression. In 'So What', the two chords (D and Eb) are not a II–III — they are two juxtaposed colours.

cours32.title: Advanced Jazz Extensions and Modern Reharmonization
cours32.subtitle: b9, #9, #11, b13 — available tensions by chord degree, Coltrane's Giant Steps and advanced substitutions for creative reharmonization.

quiz.perfect: Perfect! You have mastered altered extensions and Coltrane changes.
quiz.veryGood: Very good! A few tensions to refine — b9 vs #9, and the Giant Steps cycle.
quiz.keepGoing: Keep studying Coltrane — restart when you are ready.

narrative.maitreCardAnecdote: Coltrane composed 'Giant Steps' in 1960 — a chord progression rotating around 3 tonal centers a major third apart (B, G, Eb). The tempo was so fast that even the best pianists couldn't accompany him in rehearsals. McCoy Tyner had to learn a whole new harmonic language to keep up.
narrative.maitreCardLesson: When the progression moves too fast to think chord by chord, you need to find the geometric system underneath.
narrative.pExtensions: Altered extensions add notes beyond the seventh. On a dominant chord (V7), these extensions create specific colors — from dark tension (b9) to funky sound (#9) and the Lydian (#11). Each extension has its own source scale.
narrative.infoBoxGuideTones: Guide-tone rule: The third and seventh of a dominant chord (B and F in G7) are the essential notes — they define the chord quality and guide the resolution. Extensions are added on top without obscuring these guide-tones.
narrative.pTensionsTable: Not all extensions are stylistically appropriate on every degree. The table below lists 'available' tensions (that sound good) and those to avoid.
narrative.warnBoxExtensions: Common mistake: Stacking b9 and #9 simultaneously — they create contradictory colors (dark vs funky). Choose ONE extension color per chord based on the desired effect. On G7: b9 (Ravel, flamenco) OR #9 (Hendrix, funk) — not both.
narrative.pColtrane: John Coltrane developed a reharmonization technique based on the cycle of major thirds. Rather than staying in one key, he replaces a chord with three tonal centers a major third apart — forming a perfect triangle on the circle of fifths.
narrative.infoBoxTriangle: Coltrane's triangle: B, G, and Eb are each exactly a major third apart. These three tonics divide the octave into three equal parts. Cycling through all three in 16 bars at fast tempo creates the 'Giant Steps' effect — too fast for the ear to follow, but geometrically perfect.
narrative.pSubstitutions: Reharmonization replaces the original chords with others that maintain the same function or color, but with a different harmonic flavor.
narrative.warnBoxSubstitution: Tritone substitution — the rule: It only works on V7, not on all chords. G7 → Db7 works because they share the same guide-tones (B/C# · F/Eb). Attempting a tritone substitution on IMaj7 or IIm7 produces a non-stylistic dissonance — the characteristic note changes role.
narrative.cycleBtn: ▶ Listen to the cycle BMaj7 → GMaj7 → EbMaj7
narrative.sequenceLabel: Full Giant Steps sequence: B – G – Eb (3 tonics a major third apart)

cours27.title: Deep Functional Analysis
cours27.subtitle: Tonal hierarchies, harmonic prolongation and Schenkerian reduction — learning to see the deep structure of a work behind its surface chords.

quiz.perfect: Perfect! You have mastered deep functional analysis and Schenkerian reduction.
quiz.veryGood: Very good! A few nuances to refine on the Ursatz and prolongations.
quiz.keepGoing: Keep studying structural analysis — restart when you are ready.

narrative.maitreCardAnecdote: Schenker was convinced that all great tonal music rests on an identical deep structure — the Ursatz. Behind the apparent complexity of Bach or Beethoven, he always saw the same fundamental arch.
narrative.maitreCardLesson: Understanding a work means seeing through the surface notes down to its skeletal structure.
narrative.pHierarchies1: Tonal harmony does not treat all chords equally. Each chord belongs to a functional region — tonic (T), subdominant (SD) or dominant (D) — which determines its role in the directional logic of music.
narrative.pHierarchies2: The essential distinction: surface harmony refers to all chords visible in the score; structural harmony retains only the pillars that carry the deep tonal logic.
narrative.regionToniqueDesc: Chords of rest and stability. Share at least two notes with the tonic chord. Role: anchoring and prolongation of the tonal center.
narrative.regionSousDomDesc: Intermediate preparation chords. Create directional movement between T and D without the maximum tension of the dominant.
narrative.regionDomDesc: Maximum tension chords. Contain the tritone (leading tone + 4th) which demands resolution to I. V7 is the dominant chord par excellence.
narrative.infoBoxDirection: Directional rule: The canonical functional progression is T → SD → D → T. Every well-formed tonal progression follows this direction. The reverse movement (D → SD or SD → T directly after SD) creates functional backwards movements to avoid.
narrative.h3CouchesAnalyse: Layered analysis — surface vs deep structure
narrative.pCouchesAnalyse: The same harmonic progression can be read at three levels of depth. The more one reduces, the more the fundamental tonal structure appears.
narrative.coucheSurfaceDesc: All chords as they appear in the score — passing chords, neighbor notes, ornaments included.
narrative.coucheIntermDesc: Chords grouped by function. Ornaments and passing chords are eliminated. Only the functional pillars remain.
narrative.coucheProfondDesc: The Ursatz: fundamental structure. A single prolongation T→D→T reveals the tonal arch of the work.
narrative.pExempleSonore: Listen to this excerpt from Chorale BWV 227 in C major. The surface melody ornaments a simple deep structure: prolonged I → V → I.
narrative.warnBoxPiege: Common pitfall: Analyzing each chord in isolation, without seeing the functional region to which it belongs. Harmonic logic is directional — T→SD→D→T — not a succession of independent chords.
narrative.pUrsatz: Heinrich Schenker (1868–1935) developed a layered analysis method that reveals the deep tonal structure of every great work. At the foundation of all tonal music, he sees one same structure: the Ursatz, composed of the Urlinie (soprano) and the Bassbrechung (bass).
narrative.urlinieTexte: The Urlinie is the fundamental melodic descent of the soprano. It begins from a Kopfton (covering note: 3̂, 5̂ or 8̂) and descends diatonically to 1̂ (tonic). Three possible forms:
narrative.urlinieLi1: 3̂–2̂–1̂ (third descent) — the most common in short forms
narrative.urlinieLi2: 5̂–4̂–3̂–2̂–1̂ (fifth descent) — very common in Bach and Mozart
narrative.h3Bassbrechung: The Bassbrechung — I–V–I arch in the bass
narrative.bassbrechungTexte: The Bassbrechung is the fundamental bass arch: I–V–I. It describes the fifth movement (C→G→C) that structures tonality at any scale — from a 4-measure phrase to an entire symphony. The Bassbrechung is the universal harmonic law of tonal music.
narrative.h3MethodeReduction: Reduction method in 4 steps — Mozart K.331
narrative.step1Detail: Analyze each chord in the score and name it. Omit nothing at this step — this is the complete Vordergrund.
narrative.step1Exemple: Mozart K.331 mm.1–4: I – V6 – I – IV6 – I6/4 – V – I
narrative.step2Detail: Identify which chords belong to T (I, III, VI), SD (II, IV) or D (V, VII). Locate prolongations.
narrative.step2Exemple: T(prolonged) – SD – T(cad. 6/4) – D – T: intermediate structure
narrative.step3Detail: Locate the opening chord (I), the medial cadence (V or half-cadence), the final cadence (V–I). These are the pillars of the Bassbrechung.
narrative.step3Exemple: Mozart K.331: I (m.1) → V (m.8) → I (m.16) — Bassbrechung over 16 measures
narrative.step4Detail: Extract the fundamental soprano descent (Kopfton → 1̂) and the bass arch (I–V–I). Superimpose them: that is the Ursatz.
narrative.step4Exemple: Urlinie 3̂–2̂–1̂ (E–D–C) over Bassbrechung I–V–I = complete Ursatz
narrative.infoBoxUrsatz: The Ursatz in summary: Superimpose the Urlinie (soprano descending from 3̂/5̂/8̂ to 1̂) and the Bassbrechung (bass I–V–I). This two-voice counterpoint is the universal fundamental structure of all tonal music — every work is an elaborate prolongation of it.
narrative.warnBoxReduction: Caution: Schenkerian reduction does not simplify arbitrarily — it reveals the structural hierarchy. Never skip steps: start with the complete surface, then reduce layer by layer down to the Ursatz.

cours28.title: Musical Forms in Depth
cours28.subtitle: Binary, ternary, rondo, sonata form — anatomy of the great musical architectures, with guided analysis of Mozart, Bach and Beethoven.

quiz.perfect: Perfect! You have mastered all the major musical forms.
quiz.veryGood: Very good! A few points to consolidate on sonata form.
quiz.keepGoing: Keep studying musical forms — restart when you are ready.

narrative.maitreCardAnecdote: Beethoven stretched sonata form to its limits — the Ninth Symphony lasts 70 minutes where Mozart would take 25. He understood that form is not a container but a dramatic force in itself.
narrative.maitreCardLesson: Form is not where you put the music — it is what the music becomes.
narrative.pFormes: Musical form is the organization of discourse in time. It determines how musical ideas succeed, contrast and resolve. Each major period developed its own formal architectures.
narrative.infoBoxPrincipe: Fundamental principle: Every great form creates a tonal tension that awaits resolution. In sonata form, it is T2 at the dominant that demands a return to the tonic. Form is not a container — it is a harmonic force that unfolds through time.
narrative.formeBinaireDesc: Binary form alternates two repeated sections. Section A creates tonal instability (often moving to the dominant); section B resolves to the tonic. It is the structure of Baroque dances.
narrative.formeBinaireEx2: Allemandes and courantes from the Well-Tempered Clavier
narrative.formeTernaireTonalite: A → tonic · B → contrasting key · A' → tonic
narrative.formeTernaireDesc: Ternary form presents a theme (A), a contrasting section (B), then the return of the theme (A'). It structures the minuet, scherzo, nocturne and many Romantic pieces.
narrative.formeRondoTonalite: A always at the tonic · B, C → contrasting keys
narrative.formeRondoDesc: The rondo alternates a refrain (A) always at the tonic with contrasting episodes (B, C…). It is the typical form of Classical finales — its cyclical character suits the conclusion.
narrative.formeRondoEx1: Finale of the Pathétique Sonata op. 13 (Beethoven)
narrative.formeSonateTonalite: T1 tonic · T2 dominant → modulations → T1 + T2 at the TONIC
narrative.formeSonateDesc: Sonata form is the great instrumental form of Classicism. It creates a tonal tension (T2 at the dominant) that only the recapitulation can resolve (T2 at the tonic). It is a complete musical dramaturgy.
narrative.sonateExpTonalite: T1 at the tonic · T2 at the dominant (major) or relative major (minor)
narrative.sonateExpDetail: The exposition presents the two main themes in two distinct keys. T1 establishes the home key; a transition modulates to T2 in the dominant key (in major). This tonal tension is the driving force of the whole sonata.
narrative.sonateDevTonalite: Free modulations — FORBIDDEN to stay in the tonic
narrative.sonateDevDetail: The development fragments, combines and modulates from the material of the exposition. It must pass through several keys — that is its dramatic role. It concludes on V (dominant), preparing the return.
narrative.sonateReexTonalite: T1 at the tonic · T2 at the TONIC (fundamental resolution)
narrative.sonateReexDetail: The recapitulation restates the exposition but with T2 at the TONIC instead of the dominant. This is the harmonic resolution of the entire form. The transition is modified to stay in the tonic.
narrative.pAnalyse: Comparing works in different forms allows us to perceive how each architecture organizes harmonic and temporal space differently. These three works illustrate three distinct formal logics.
narrative.oeuvre1Desc: This prelude is a continuous harmonic progression in arpeggios — no distinct themes, no formal binary or ternary structure. Bach unfolds a succession of arpeggiated chords from I to I, with remarkable harmonic enrichments (II7, V7). It is a pre-Classical form close to the toccata.
narrative.oeuvre2Desc: K. 331 is unique among Mozart's sonatas: its first movement is a theme and variations — not a sonata form. The theme in A major is followed by six variations that transform its rhythm, melody and harmony. The famous Rondo alla Turca is the finale of this sonata.
narrative.oeuvre3Desc: The Pathétique is distinguished by a slow introduction (Grave) in C minor that precedes the Allegro di molto e con brio. Beethoven brings this Grave back in the middle of the development and at the end of the recapitulation — an original formal expansion that goes beyond the classic sonata scheme.
narrative.infoBoxRetenir: Key takeaway: Bach (harmonic arpeggiation) → no distinct themes, contrapuntal logic. Mozart K. 331 (theme and variations) → a single transformed theme. Beethoven op. 13 (enriched sonata form) → two themes, harmonic tension, and return of the opening Grave in the development.

cours29.title: Comparative Repertoire Analysis
cours29.subtitle: Baroque, Classical, Romantic, Impressionist — 5 historical periods and their harmonic signatures, illustrated by one melody harmonized 5 different ways.

quiz.perfect: Perfect! You can precisely identify styles and historical harmonic periods.
quiz.veryGood: Very good! A few stylistic nuances to refine.
quiz.keepGoing: Keep comparing styles — restart when you are ready.

narrative.maitreCardAnecdote: Debussy refused to be called 'impressionist'. He preferred 'French musician'. But when asked about his harmonic rules, he replied: 'My pleasure is my rule.' Paradoxically, his apparent freedom rests on a perfect knowledge of everything he was transgressing.
narrative.maitreCardLesson: To break free from the rules, you must first master them perfectly.
narrative.pEvolution: From the Baroque to Impressionism, harmonic language passed through 5 major stylistic periods. Each redefines the relationship to dissonance, resolution and tonal functions. Click a period to explore it.
narrative.infoBoxCritere: Identification criterion: what is the relationship to the dominant? Bach: mandatory — Mozart: assertive and economical — Schubert: enriched — Wagner: suspended — Debussy: absent.
narrative.pComparaison: The melody C–E–G–C is harmonized here in 5 different styles. Same melody, radically distinct harmonic worlds. Compare the treatment of the dominant, dissonances and colors for each period.
narrative.harmonisationBaroqueDesc: Strict counterpoint, assertive V7–I cadence. The melody is supported by an independent counterpoint.
narrative.harmonisationClassiqueDesc: I–IV–V–I economical and clear. The Alberti bass accompanies discreetly.
narrative.harmonisationRomantiqueDesc: I–bVI–V–I with chromaticism. The borrowing from the minor creates an unexpected dark color.
narrative.harmonisationPostRomDesc: I–III–bVII–I with tonal ambiguity. The tonic arrives but after an unexpected detour.
narrative.harmonisationImpressDesc: Parallel 9th chords. No functional resolution — color takes precedence over tension.
narrative.infoBoxObserver: To observe: compare the bass on the 2nd chord of each harmonization. Bach descends toward the dominant G. Debussy rises in parallel motion. The difference lies there — in the logic of bass movement.

cours26.title: DEM Harmonization: Given Bass and Given Soprano
cours26.subtitle: The two fundamental exercises of the DEM exam — step-by-step method, guided exercises with detailed corrections.

quiz.perfect: Perfect! You have mastered DEM harmonization techniques.
quiz.veryGood: Very good! A few points to consolidate on bass or soprano.
quiz.keepGoing: Keep practicing harmonization — restart when you are ready.

narrative.maitreCardAnecdote: César Franck was renowned for his ability to harmonize any bass at sight impeccably. His students (Duparc, d'Indy, Chausson) testified that he instantly saw all the harmonic possibilities of a given bass line.
narrative.maitreCardLesson: A well-harmonized bass already tells the whole story — the upper voices just need to follow.
narrative.pBasse: The given bass is one of the two major harmonization exercises in the DEM exam. Each bass note reveals the chord position — you must choose which one is most relevant according to functional logic.
narrative.methodeBasse1Detail: Analyze the key signature and confirm via the final cadence. Distinguish major and minor (presence of the leading tone?).
narrative.methodeBasse2Detail: Root → root position. Third → 1st inversion. Fifth → 2nd inversion (rare, cadential). Foreign note → do not place a chord.
narrative.methodeBasse3Detail: Each bass note may correspond to several chords compatible with the key — list the options.
narrative.methodeBasse4Detail: Follow T → SD → D → T. Avoid backward movements (IV–V–IV). Prepare the final cadence with V or I6/4–V.
narrative.methodeBasse5Detail: Avoid parallel fifths and octaves. Double the root in root position, the third in 1st inversion. Favor contrary motion.
narrative.infoBoxCadence: Critical rule — the cadential 6/4: The 6/4 is never placed on a weak beat. It appears only on the strong beat (2nd or 4th beat), followed obligatorily by V then I. Any other use of 2nd inversion is extremely rare.
narrative.exB1Desc: Harmonize this bass in C major. First identify the position of each note (root, third or fifth).
narrative.exB1Notes: C – G – A – F – G – C
narrative.exB1Analyse: I – V6 – VI – IV – V – I (perfect final cadence)
narrative.exB1Indice: G in the bass = third of E (V6) or root of V. Here, G after C suggests V6 because A follows — VI is more logical after V6 than after V.
narrative.exB2Desc: Harmonize this bass in G major. Watch for the leading tone (F#) and inversions.
narrative.exB2Notes: G – D – E – C – D – G
narrative.exB2Analyse: I – V – VI – IV – V – I (classic symmetrical structure)
narrative.exB2Indice: D is the root of V or the fifth of I. Here, after G (I), D → E suggests a V–VI movement (deceptive cadence) before the conclusion IV–V–I.
narrative.exB3Desc: Bass in harmonic D minor. The leading tone C# must appear before the final D. Watch for bVI (Bb).
narrative.exB3Notes: D – A – Bb – G – C# – D
narrative.exB3Analyse: Im – V – bVI – IVm – V(harm.) – Im (perfect cadence in harmonic minor)
narrative.exB3Indice: C# in the bass = VII#6 (1st inversion of VII# = leading tone of harmonic minor). This V6 rises to Im by semitone — maximum tension before resolution.
narrative.pSoprano: Harmonizing a given soprano first requires identifying real notes from foreign notes — then constructing a coherent bass and inner voices beneath the melody.
narrative.methodeSoprano1Detail: The melody reveals the key — locate characteristic accidentals (leading tone, 6th in minor) and the final note.
narrative.methodeSoprano2Detail: What chord position can it occupy? Root, third, fifth, or seventh of the chord?
narrative.methodeSoprano3Detail: Short or ornamental notes: passing (between two chord tones), neighbor (around a note), suspension (resolved dissonance), anticipation.
narrative.methodeSoprano4Detail: For each real soprano note, list the chords that can contain it (maximum 2–3 options).
narrative.methodeSoprano5Titre: Build the bass and inner voices
narrative.methodeSoprano5Detail: Choose chord positions according to the desired harmonic rhythm. Prefer root position at cadences. Vary inversions in the body of the phrase.
narrative.notePassageDef: Stepwise between two chord tones, unaccented. Ascending or descending.
narrative.broderieDef: Around a chord tone — goes up and comes back down (or the reverse). Diatonic or chromatic.
narrative.retardDef: Strikes on the strong beat in place of the chord tone — resolves downward.
narrative.anticipationDef: Anticipates the next note before the harmonic resolution — short and unaccented.
narrative.warnBoxSoprano: Golden rule: Never harmonize a foreign note. First identify all passing notes, neighbors and suspensions — then harmonize only the real notes. A chord on a neighbor note creates a fatal error in the exam.
narrative.exS1Desc: Harmonize this soprano in C major. All notes are real (no foreign notes).
narrative.exS1Analyse: I6 – V6 – I – V – I (or I6 – II6 – I – V – I depending on the chosen harmonic rhythm)
narrative.exS1Indice: E in the soprano can be the third of I (root position C–E–G) or the root of III (rarely). Starting with I6 (third in soprano) is elegant.
narrative.exS2Desc: This soprano contains a foreign note. Identify it before harmonizing.
narrative.exS2Notes: G – F# – G – E – D – C
narrative.exS2Analyse: I – (passing note) – I – I6 or VI – V – I · F# = chromatic passing note between G and E
narrative.exS2Indice: F# is too short and non-harmonic to receive a chord — it is a chromatic passing note between G and E. Do not harmonize F#: maintain the G chord (I) on the first two notes.

faq.q: Do I need a background in music theory?
faq.a: No. Lesson 1 starts from the acoustic origins of the scale — no prerequisites required. Harmonia suits complete beginners as well as musicians who want to formalise what they already know.

faq.q: How long does the programme take to master?
faq.a: Level 1 (9 lessons) represents roughly 30 to 50 hours of work depending on your pace and starting point. With an hour of regular practice a day, expect 6 to 8 weeks.

faq.q: Are the exercises suitable for every instrument?
faq.a: Yes. Tonal harmony is a universal language. The exercises train abstract harmonic understanding — applicable to any instrument, or to composition.

faq.q: Can I cancel my subscription at any time?
faq.a: Yes, with no commitment. Cancellation takes effect at the end of the current period. On the annual plan you keep access until the end of the year you paid for.
Yes. Harmonia is under active development — new exercises, lessons and features are added regularly. Pro subscribers get every update.
