cours33.title: Classical Writing: Fugue and Chorale
cours33.subtitle: Four-voice Bach-style chorale and fugue structure — the summit of strict counterpoint and polyphonic writing.

quiz.perfect: Perfect! You have mastered Bach chorale and fugue structure.
quiz.veryGood: Very good! Review the chorale rules and fugue anatomy.
quiz.keepGoing: Keep going — try again when you're ready.

narrative.maitreCardAnecdote: Bach composed his fugues mentally before writing them down. The Art of Fugue, his musical testament, stops in the middle of a bar — the day of his death. His name (B-A-C-H in German notation: Bb-A-C-B) is the last thematic motif he wrote in the unfinished fugue.
narrative.maitreCardLesson: The fugue is not a form — it is a way of thinking in multiple voices simultaneously.
narrative.pChoral: The four-voice chorale (SATB) is the fundamental exercise of classical writing. Bach harmonized ~400 Lutheran chorale melodies — these harmonizations are the absolute model of the genre. Each voice is an independent melodic line; their combination produces the harmony.
narrative.infoBoxSATB: The 4 SATB voices: Soprano (high female voice, given chorale melody) · Alto (low female voice) · Tenor (high male voice, sounds an octave lower than written) · Bass (low male voice, harmonic foundation). The goal: 4 independent lines that sound good together at every moment.
narrative.step1Desc: Find the main key, identify phrase endings (textual punctuation = musical cadence)
narrative.step2Desc: The bass establishes the harmony. Prefer root positions, fifths in the bass for strong cadences
narrative.step3Desc: Fill the inner voices while avoiding errors. Contrary motion preferred over parallel voices
narrative.step4Desc: Parallel fifths/octaves? Leading tone resolved? 7th descending? Correct doublings?
narrative.warnBoxChoral: Most common mistake: Moving the leading tone up to the dominant instead of the tonic. B in C major (leading tone) moving to D instead of C — this is a serious stylistic error. The leading tone MUST rise by a semitone to the tonic, without exception in Bach style.
narrative.h3ExemplesAccords: Examples of SATB chords in C major
narrative.pFugue: The fugue is a form of counterpoint where multiple voices enter successively with the same theme (subject), then develop it by weaving independent lines. It is not a 'form' in the sense of the sonata — it is a contrapuntal process.
narrative.infoBoxAnatomie: Anatomy of a fugue: Exposition (successive subject/answer entries) → Development (episodes, entries in other keys) → Stretto (overlapping entries) → Coda (tonic pedal, final cadence). The structure follows the logic of counterpoint, not a predefined form.
narrative.infoBoxArtFugue: The Art of Fugue BWV 1080: 14 fugues (Contrapuncti) and 4 canons, all built on the same subject in C minor. Contrapunctus XIV stops in the middle of a bar — Bach died before completing it. The last note Bach wrote: a B (the B-A-C-H motif ends on B in French notation).
narrative.warnBoxFugue: Common mistake: Analyzing the fugue as a succession of chords. The fugue is a fabric of independent melodic lines — harmony results from their meeting, it is not planned chord by chord. Analyze each voice separately before analyzing their intersection.

cours34.title: Composing for the Screen: Film Harmony
cours34.subtitle: Harmonic emotion map, leitmotif and transformation — Morricone, Williams, Zimmer and the art of guiding the viewer's emotion.

quiz.perfect: Perfect! You have mastered film harmony and the leitmotif.
quiz.veryGood: Very good! Review the emotion map and transformation techniques.
quiz.keepGoing: Keep going — try again when you're ready.

narrative.maitreCardAnecdote: Morricone composed his music BEFORE filming with Sergio Leone. Actors sometimes played on set listening to the music in their earpiece — the melody dictated the rhythm of their performance. For 'Il Buono, il Brutto, il Cattivo', he invented an orchestration with human whistling, coyote voice and jaw harp. Recognizable in 2 seconds anywhere in the world.
narrative.maitreCardLesson: Film music does not tell what we see — it says what we feel watching what we see.
narrative.pEmotions: Every emotion in cinema has its harmonic color. Great composers (Williams, Morricone, Zimmer, Herrmann) use a precise harmonic vocabulary to guide the viewer's emotion — often without them realizing it. This map is their toolbox.
narrative.infoBoxPrincipe: Fundamental principle: Emotion in film music comes from harmonic color (which scale, which chord), timbre (which instrument) and dynamics (which volume) — not harmonic complexity. A simple chord well orchestrated is more powerful than a complex harmony poorly played.
narrative.outil2: Open fifth + fanfare brass at the octave
narrative.orchDesc0: Brass in the low register (tuba, trombone) evoke threat. High strings in the high register = tension.
narrative.orchDesc1: Plucked strings create a lightness and softness that the bow cannot. Ideal for comedy or carefree moments.
narrative.orchDesc2: A motif that repeats unchanged creates a sense of imminence and inevitability — tension rising without resolution.
narrative.orchDesc3: Silence after loud music is the most powerful tool. The sudden absence of sound creates an emotional shock stronger than the music itself.
narrative.warnBoxEmotions: Common mistake: Using the same harmonic register for all emotions. If sadness sounds like heroism, the viewer does not know how to react. The harmonic emotion map is a precision tool — each color has a distinct narrative function.
narrative.pLeitmotif: A leitmotif is a musical motif (melody, harmony, rhythm) associated with a character, object or idea in a work. It can be transformed harmonically to reflect the narrative evolution of the character — without changing the notes.
narrative.infoBoxOrigine: Origin: Richard Wagner invented the leitmotif in his operas of the tetralogy Der Ring des Nibelungen (1876). Each character, magic object or feeling has its motif. John Williams applied this technique to cinema with equivalent mastery in Star Wars.
narrative.lm0Description: Darth Vader's theme — hammered brass in minor, warlike dotted rhythm. The minor third and diminished fifth at the end evoke oppression and threat.
narrative.lm0Transformation: The same theme played by harp or soft strings = Vader's hidden vulnerability — double effect without changing a single note.
narrative.lm1Description: Heroic theme in D major, initial ascending fifth = aspiration and grandeur. The Lydian mode appears in the fantasy passages (Tatooine sunset).
narrative.lm1Transformation: In minor mode or slow tempo = melancholy, loss, nostalgia — the same melody says the opposite.
narrative.lm2Nom: Il Buono, il Brutto, il Cattivo
narrative.lm2Film: The Good, the Bad and the Ugly (1966)
narrative.lm2Description: Aeolian mode with human whistling, voice imitating a coyote, electric jaw harp, chimes. The harmonic color is simple; the absolutely unique orchestration creates the sonic identity of the spaghetti western.
narrative.lm3Description: Only 4 chords — C–G–Am–F — repeated 18 times over 4 minutes. The progressive orchestration (piano → strings → full ensemble) creates all the tension and emotion. Lesson: harmonic complexity is not the engine of emotion.
narrative.transDesc0: The same melody in minor mode signals threat, corruption or the character's loss. The viewer recognizes the theme and feels the change instantly.
narrative.transDesc1: Hammered brass → harp or soft strings. Same melody, opposite emotion. Vader's Imperial March on harp = his human vulnerability.
narrative.transDesc2: The theme in 2–3 note fragments creates anxiety. The ear seeks to complete the melody — the composer refuses to finish it.
narrative.transDesc3: Doubled note values (slower = epic grandeur) or halved (faster = urgency and danger). Contrapuntal technique applied to cinema.
narrative.infoBoxMorricone: Morricone and Leone: Morricone composed BEFORE filming. Leone showed the music to actors on set — they played while listening. The music dictated the rhythm and emotion of the performance, the reverse of the classical method. This unique collaboration explains the perfect fusion of image and sound in their films.
narrative.warnBoxLeitmotif: Advanced technique: A leitmotif in major that becomes minor is more powerful than a new sad theme. Why? The viewer already has an emotional connection to the original theme — its transformation amplifies the impact. Creating a leitmotif is investing in an emotional resource the composer can exploit throughout the film.

cours35.title: Advanced Jazz: Reharmonization and Improvisation
cours35.subtitle: Advanced substitutions, chord scales and professional voicings — Miles Davis, Bill Evans and the art of creative reharmonization.

quiz.perfect: Perfect! You have mastered advanced reharmonization and jazz voicings.
quiz.veryGood: Very good! Review the substitutions and chord scales.
quiz.keepGoing: Keep going — try again when you're ready.

narrative.maitreCardAnecdote: Kind of Blue (1959) was recorded in two sessions without rehearsal — the musicians discovered the modal charts in the studio. Miles gave them a scale and a color, not chords. The result: the best-selling jazz album in history. Miles Davis said: 'Don't play what's there — play what's not there.'
narrative.maitreCardLesson: Harmonic sophistication in jazz does not come from complexity — it comes from knowing exactly what to put in and what to leave out.
narrative.pReharmonisation: Reharmonising means replacing the chords of an existing chart with other chords under the same melody. The absolute constraint: every melodic note must remain compatible with the new chord (root, 3rd, 5th, 7th or available extension — never an avoid note).
narrative.infoBoxGuideTones: Guide-tones: The third and seventh of the chord are the notes that define its harmonic color. These are the essential notes never to clash with the melody during a reharmonisation. If the melody is a semitone above or below a guide-tone, the chord is poorly chosen.
narrative.sub0Principe: I ↔ III ↔ VI — chords sharing 2 notes in the same key
narrative.sub0Exemple: CMaj7 can be replaced by Am7 (same key, 2 common notes: E, G)
narrative.sub1Principe: V7 replaced by the dominant chord a tritone lower — shared guide-tones
narrative.sub1Exemple: G7 → Db7: B (3rd of G7) = C# (7th of Db7), F (7th of G7) = Eb (3rd of Db7)
narrative.sub2Principe: Borrow chords from the parallel mode — bVI, bVII, bIII from the aeolian scale
narrative.sub2Exemple: In C major: Fm (parallel minor), Ab Maj, Bb Maj — dark and unexpected color
narrative.sub3Principe: Replacement by a chord a third away — major or minor, different mode
narrative.sub3Exemple: CMaj7 → EMaj (major third) or AbMaj (major sixth) — cinematic color
narrative.h3ChordScales: Chord scales — which scale over which chord?
narrative.cs3Gamme: Altered = 7th mode of Ab melodic minor
narrative.warnBoxReharmo: Classic mistake: Using the tritone substitution on all chords. It only works on dominant V7 chords — because that is the only time the guide-tones (3rd and 7th) are shared with the substitute. Applying it to a IMaj7 or IIm7 produces a non-stylistic dissonance.
narrative.pVoicings: A voicing is the arrangement of a chord's notes in register. For the same chord, there are dozens of possible voicings — each with its color, context of use and sonic effect. Mastering voicings distinguishes the harmonist from the ordinary pianist.
narrative.infoBoxVoicings: Fundamental principle: There is no 'correct' voicing in absolute terms — there are voicings suited to each context. The shell voicing to accompany a voice, the drop 2 for a richer texture, the Evans voicing for the jazz trio, the quartal for modal color.
narrative.v0Description: Root + 3rd + 7th without fifth. The skeleton of the chord — stripped down, leaves space for melody and bass.
narrative.v0Construction: CMaj7: C–E–B (fifth G omitted). G7: G–B–F. Dm7: D–F–C.
narrative.v0Style: Voice accompaniment, double bass plays the root
narrative.v1Description: Closed chord whose 2nd voice (from the top) drops an octave — opening the register.
narrative.v1Construction: CMaj7 closed: C–E–G–B → Drop 2: C–G–B–E (G drops an octave)
narrative.v1Style: Jazz guitarists, pianists — wider and more natural register
narrative.v2Description: Root omitted (played by the double bassist). 3rd–7th–9th in a light, airy voicing.
narrative.v2Construction: CMaj9 Evans: E–B–D (3rd–7th–9th). The root C is implied.
narrative.v3Description: Chord built in stacked fourths. Miles Davis and Bill Evans for 'So What'.
narrative.v3Construction: D–G–C–F–A: four successive fourths + third at the top
narrative.v3Style: Modal jazz, open sound without defined major/minor color
narrative.v4Description: Left hand plays 3rd–7th (+ optional extensions), right hand plays melody/solo.
narrative.v4Construction: G7 left hand: B–F–A (3–7–13) or B–F–Ab (3–7–b13 altered)
narrative.v4Style: Pianists accompanying a soloist or singer
narrative.warnBoxVoicings: Common mistake: Confusing chord scale and mode. A chord scale is always tied to a specific chord in a precise harmonic context. CMaj7 in C major uses ionian by default — but if you want the lydian color (#11), you choose lydian. The context (tonal or modal) determines the chord scale, not the chart alone.

cours36.title: Debussy and Ravel Harmony: Impressionism in Depth
cours36.subtitle: Whole-tone scale, planing, polychords, neoclassical modality — the two opposing logics of French impressionism.

quiz.perfect: Perfect! You have mastered the impressionist techniques of Debussy and Ravel.
quiz.veryGood: Very good! Review planing, the long pedal point and polychords.
quiz.keepGoing: Keep going — try again when you're ready.

narrative.maitreCardAnecdote: Satie invented 'furniture music' (1920) — music meant to blend with ambient noise, like a sonic piece of furniture. At its premiere, the audience politely sat and listened. An outraged Satie stormed through the hall shouting: 'Talk! Move! Don't listen!' He prefigured Brian Eno and ambient music by 50 years.
narrative.maitreCardLesson: Music can exist without tension or resolution — harmonic colour is an end in itself.
narrative.debussyP: Debussy dissolved the classical tonal system not by rejecting it, but by circumventing it. He replaced harmonic functions (T/SD/D) with sound colours — a chord no longer has a role, it has a texture. Tension-resolution gives way to atmosphere.
narrative.debussyInfoBox: Fundamental principle: In Debussy, harmonic progression follows the melody and the sonic image — not functional logic. Analysing Debussy using T/SD/D functions is like applying chess rules to dice. The analytical framework is inappropriate.
narrative.debussyH3Analyser: How to analyse an impressionist piece
narrative.debussyWarnBox: Classic mistake: Looking for V→I cadences in Debussy. Debussy systematically avoids them — it is the very principle of his aesthetic. The Prelude to the Afternoon of a Faun (110 bars) contains no classical dominant-tonic cadence. The harmony 'floats' without ever resolving.
narrative.tech_d0_principe: 6 whole tones with no semitone — C–D–E–F#–G#–A#
narrative.tech_d0_effet: Total floating: without a leading tone, no note pulls toward the next. All chords are augmented.
narrative.tech_d1_principe: 8 notes alternating tone and semitone — C–D–Eb–F–F#–Ab–A–B
narrative.tech_d1_effet: Ambiguous, symmetrical colour — tonality is impossible to establish (the scale repeats at a minor third).
narrative.tech_d2_principe: All voices move in the same rhythm with the same chord type transposed
narrative.tech_d2_effet: The chord moves as a sound block — parallel fifths are intentional, creating the characteristic sliding effect.
narrative.tech_d3_principe: Stationary bass (held note) while the harmony changes freely above
narrative.tech_d3_effet: Temporal suspension — harmonic time stops in the bass. Medieval technique (organum) reinvented.
narrative.tech_d4_principe: Root + fifth chord with no third — neither major nor minor
narrative.tech_d4_effet: Archaic, timeless purity, evoking plainchant and bells (La Cathédrale Engloutie).
narrative.step1_desc: Whole-tone (6 whole tones), octatonic (tone-semitone), pentatonic, or church mode?
narrative.step2_desc: Planing? Pedal point? Open fifths? Parallel homorhythm?
narrative.step3_desc: Chord quality, extensions present, register. No harmonic function.
narrative.step4_desc: Colour changes often come from register and volume, not chord progressions.
narrative.ravelP: Ravel shares with Debussy the impressionist surface — coloured chords, extensions, modes — but his logic is the opposite. Ravel described himself as a 'watchmaker': every chord is calculated, every voicing measured. The Pavane, the Bolero, the Valses nobles are architectural constructions disguised as reveries.
narrative.ravelInfoBox: Essential difference: Debussy creates intuitively through images and sonic colours. Ravel calculates with near-mathematical rigour. Same impressionist surface, opposite compositional logics. Debussy himself clearly distinguished between them and refused to be associated with Ravel.
narrative.tech_r0_principe: Two complete chords stacked — C Maj + D Maj = hybrid cluster
narrative.tech_r0_effet: Dense, unprecedented harmonic colour — both chords remain recognisable but create something new.
narrative.tech_r1_effet: Luminous softness — less 'jazzy' than Maj7, more 'naive' and tender. Signature of Ravel's style.
narrative.tech_r2_effet: Openness and clarity — the chord seems to aspire upward. Very different from Maj9 (which also adds the 7th).
narrative.tech_r3_principe: Ancient modes (Dorian, Phrygian, Lydian) in a modern language
narrative.tech_r3_effet: Modal colour without archaism — the Dorian mode on G gives a particular light absent from harmonic minor.
narrative.tech_r4_principe: A single chord (C7) for 15 minutes — tension comes from progressive orchestration
narrative.tech_r4_effet: Demonstration that harmonic progression is not the only driver of tension — timbre and dynamics suffice.
narrative.satieP: Satie precedes Debussy and Ravel and prefigures minimalism by 50 years. His Gymnopédies (1888) use major 7th chords in slow oscillation — two colours alternating without ever progressing dramatically. His Vexations (1893, discovered after his death) anticipate Steve Reich: a 52-second piece to be played 840 times in a row.
narrative.ravelWarnBox: Common mistake: Believing Debussy and Ravel are the same composer with the same style. Their surfaces are similar (impressionism, colours) but their methods are opposite. Ravel can write in strict classical forms (Piano Sonatine, String Quartet) with an impressionist language. Debussy avoids classical forms. This difference is fundamental.

cours37.title: Advanced Analysis: Schenker and Motivic Analysis
cours37.subtitle: Ursatz, Urlinie, 4 reduction levels and motivic analysis — seeing the deep structure of a work beyond its surface.

quiz.perfect: Perfect! You have mastered Schenkerian analysis and motivic analysis.
quiz.veryGood: Very good! Review the 4 reduction levels and motivic transformations.
quiz.keepGoing: Keep going — try again when you're ready.

narrative.maitreCardAnecdote: Schoenberg is famous for twelve-tone music, but he was also a major analyst and theorist. He developed the concept of Grundgestalt (basic shape) — a 2-to-6-note motif from which an entire work unfolds. His manual 'Fundamentals of Musical Composition' is still used in conservatories. Paradox: the father of atonality was obsessed with the motivic coherence that only tonal music can establish clearly.
narrative.maitreCardLesson: Analysis does not explain why music is beautiful — it reveals the framework beneath the surface. It is a tool, not a value.
narrative.schenkerP: Heinrich Schenker (1868–1935) developed a theory according to which all tonal music is an elaboration of a simple fundamental structure — the Ursatz. His approach reveals the layers of meaning hidden beneath the surface of music.
narrative.schenkerInfoBox: The Ursatz: The Urlinie (descending melodic line from the 5th, 3rd, or 8th degree to the tonic) supported by the Bassbrechung (bass arpeggiation I–V–I). All tonal music is a prolongation and elaboration of this Ursatz. In C major: G–F–E–D–C (soprano) / C–G–C (bass).
narrative.n0desc: The complete musical surface: all chords, ornaments, melodies, passing notes, neighbour notes, arpeggios. This is the music as performed.
narrative.n1desc: Reduction eliminating surface ornaments. Only the main harmonic functions and prolongations remain.
narrative.n2desc: Reduction showing only tonal regions and major harmonic prolongations.
narrative.n3desc: The fundamental structure (Ursatz): Urlinie + Bassbrechung. The harmonic framework of the entire work in a few notes.
narrative.o0desc: Connects two structural notes by stepwise motion: C–D–E, the D disappears at the reduction level
narrative.o1desc: Leaves a structural note, moves one step away, returns: C–D–C, the D is a neighbour note
narrative.o2desc: Unprepared foreign note on a strong beat, resolved by descending stepwise motion
narrative.o3desc: Resolution of a note is delayed — the non-structural note holds on the strong beat and resolves afterwards
narrative.schenkerWarn: Classic mistake: Confusing Schenkerian reduction with simplification. Reduction does not remove notes "at random" — it identifies structural notes according to precise criteria (metric position, duration, harmonic role). Ornamental notes are not less important: they are the expressive surface. Reduction reveals structure; it does not judge value.
narrative.motiviqueP: Motivic analysis identifies the melodic cells of 2 to 6 notes that form the raw material of a work, then tracks their transformations. This level of analysis reveals the organic unity of a composition — how everything derives from very little.
narrative.beethInfoBox: Beethoven — 5th Symphony: The opening motif (G–G–G–Eb) is not just the beginning of the first movement. This rhythmic (3 eighth notes + quarter note) and melodic (descending minor third) motif organises all 4 movements — in the 2nd movement theme, in the 3rd movement fanfares, in the 4th movement finale transition. This is the very definition of organic unity.
narrative.beethDesc: Two simultaneous dimensions: rhythm (short–short–short–long) and melody (descending minor third). Both are inseparable in analysis. In the 2nd movement the rhythm is transformed, but the melodic third persists.
narrative.t0desc: The motif transposed to another degree — intervals are preserved
narrative.t0ex: Beethoven 5th: motif repeated from bar 2 a tone lower
narrative.t1desc: Intervals reversed — what went up now goes down and vice versa
narrative.t1ex: Bach: inversions of the subject in fugues
narrative.t2desc: The motif in doubled values — slower, more solemn
narrative.t2ex: In a higher register to mark the augmentation
narrative.t3desc: Only the first 2–3 notes of the motif — creates unease and urgency
narrative.t3ex: Beethoven 5th: development fragments the 4 notes into 2 + 2
narrative.s1desc: 2 to 6 notes appearing at the start and repeating. A distinctive interval or rhythm is the key.
narrative.s2desc: Mark every appearance of the motif in the score — transpositions and transformations included.
narrative.s3desc: Inversion, augmentation, diminution, fragmentation, sequence. How does the motif develop?
narrative.s4desc: Does the motif create exposition, development, climax? What narrative role does it play?
narrative.brahmsInfoBox: Brahms and motivic unity: In his quartets and symphonies, Brahms often derives the entire work from a single interval (a second or a third). This generating interval appears in the melody, the bass, the transitions, the counter-melodies — always recognisable, always transformed. Brahms's motivic analysis reveals a thematic density comparable to Bach's.
narrative.motiviqueWarn: Common mistake: Analysing each surface chord without seeking the deep structure. Saying "C–G–F–C" says nothing about structural significance. Motivic analysis complements harmonic analysis: on one side the vertical structure (chords), on the other the horizontal structure (motif and its destiny). Together they reveal compositional logic.

cours38.title: Non-Chord Tones
cours38.subtitle: Passing tone, neighbor tone, appoggiatura, suspension, escape tone, anticipation and pedal point — the melodic ornamentation of harmony.

quiz.perfect: Perfect! You identify and use every non-chord tone.
quiz.veryGood: Very good! A few subtleties left to consolidate (suspension, escape tone, appoggiatura).
quiz.keepGoing: Keep going — review the definitions and listen to the examples again before retrying.

narrative.maitreCardAnecdote: Théodore Dubois, director of the Paris Conservatoire and author of a Treatise on Harmony that trained generations of musicians, classified non-chord tones with a watchmaker's precision. For him, telling an appoggiatura from a suspension, or a neighbor tone from a passing tone, was no game of labels: it was understanding how a single note, foreign to the chord, could by itself color, tense or soften an entire phrase. His students first learned to spot them in Bach before daring to write any themselves.
narrative.maitreCardLesson: A non-chord tone is never a mistake: the chord decides, and the ear is the judge.
narrative.defP1: A non-chord tone is a note that does not belong to the chord heard at that precise moment. It ornaments the melodic line, creating motion and expression without altering the actual harmony.
narrative.defInfoBox: The principle: at every instant, some notes are chord tones (they make up the chord), others are non-chord tones (they ornament it). Telling one from the other is the key to all harmonic analysis.
narrative.defP2: Non-chord tones are classified by three criteria: the beat on which they fall (strong or weak), the way one approaches them (by step or by leap) and the way one leaves them (resolution). These three criteria are enough to name every non-chord tone.
narrative.defTipBox: Remember: most non-chord tones are unprepared and fall on a weak beat. Two major exceptions: the appoggiatura (strong beat) and the suspension (strong beat AND prepared).
narrative.typesP1: Here are the most common non-chord tones, apart from the suspension (covered in its own section). Each is defined by its place on the beat and by the way it is approached and resolved.
narrative.typesClickHint: Click a non-chord tone to hear it and read its definition.
narrative.typePassageDesc: It links two different chord tones by step, on a weak beat. It may be diatonic or chromatic. Example: C → D → E, where the D (non-chord tone) links C and E (chord tones).
narrative.typeBroderieDesc: It leaves a chord tone by step, then returns to it, on a weak beat. The upper neighbor rises a step; the lower neighbor falls. Example: E → F → E (upper neighbor of E).
narrative.typeAppogiatureDesc: A non-chord tone placed on the strong beat, approached by leap or by step, then resolved by step (most often downward) onto a chord tone. It is unprepared — that is what sets it apart from the suspension. Example: F (on the strong beat) → E.
narrative.typeEchappeeDesc: It leaves a chord tone by step, then "escapes" by a leap in the opposite direction onto a tone of the next chord. Example: C → D (by step) → B (downward leap in the opposite direction).
narrative.typePassageAccDesc: This is a passing tone that, exceptionally, falls on the strong beat. Connected by stepwise motion on both sides, it resembles the appoggiatura but keeps its nature as a passing tone.
narrative.typeAnticipationDesc: It sounds in advance a note that will belong to the next chord: it arrives too early, over the chord still in progress, creating a brief dissonance before the harmony changes.
narrative.typesWarnBox: Do not confuse: the neighbor tone returns to its starting note, whereas the passing tone links two different notes. The appoggiatura arrives by leap on the strong beat; the escape tone leaves by leap.
narrative.retardP1: The suspension is the only non-chord tone that requires preparation. A consonant chord tone is held over (tied or repeated) as the harmony changes: it then becomes dissonant on the strong beat, before resolving downward by step.
narrative.retardInfoBox: What sets the suspension apart from the appoggiatura: both strike on the strong beat and resolve downward by step. But the suspension is prepared (heard first as a consonance), whereas the appoggiatura is approached freely.
narrative.retardStepsH3: The three stages of the suspension
narrative.retardStepsHint: Click each stage to see its details.
narrative.retardStepPrepDesc: The note is first heard as a consonance, in the same voice, within the preceding chord. It is this preparation that "legitimizes" the dissonance to come.
narrative.retardStepPercDesc: The harmony changes, but the note is held over: it becomes dissonant and strikes on the strong beat. This is the peak of tension of the suspension.
narrative.retardStepResDesc: The dissonant note descends by step onto a chord tone. The resolution is almost always downward.
narrative.retardAudioLabel: Listen to a 4-3 suspension on the C chord
narrative.retardAudioDesc: On the C chord (C–E–G), the suspended F strikes in place of the E (a dissonance of a fourth), then steps down onto the E: this is the 4-3 suspension.
narrative.retard43Desc: The fourth (dissonant) steps down onto the third of the chord. The most frequent suspension pattern.
narrative.retard76Desc: The seventh (dissonant) steps down onto the sixth (consonant).
narrative.retard98Desc: The ninth (dissonant) steps down onto the octave (the root).
narrative.retard23Desc: Suspension in the bass: the suspended bass forms a second with an upper voice, then steps down to give a consonant third (or tenth).
narrative.pedaleP1: The pedal point is a sustained note — most often the tonic or the dominant, in the bass — held while the harmony moves above it. It may become momentarily dissonant with the passing chords, without ever moving.
narrative.pedaleInfoBox: Tonic pedal: often at the end of a piece, it anchors the conclusion. Dominant pedal: often before a recapitulation, it creates a long tension and a strong expectation of resolution.
narrative.pedaleP2: Unlike other non-chord tones, the pedal point does not ornament a single note but holds beneath an entire progression. It is a non-chord tone by its duration and by the passing dissonances it generates.
narrative.pedaleAudioDesc: The low C is held while the upper voices move from E–G to F–A and then return to E–G: the harmony changes, the pedal remains.
narrative.emploiP1: At the DEM, non-chord tones come into play in two fundamental exercises: spotting non-chord tones to analyze a work, and using them to realize a given melody or a given bass with naturalness and style.
narrative.emploiTipBox: Spotting method: for each note, ask whether it belongs to the chord. If not, look at the beat (strong / weak), the approach (by step / by leap) and the resolution. These three clues name it.
narrative.emploiStepsH3: Spotting a non-chord tone in four questions
narrative.emploiStep1: Does it belong to the chord? If yes, it is a chord tone — not a non-chord tone.
narrative.emploiStep2: On which beat does it fall? Strong beat → appoggiatura or suspension. Weak beat → passing tone, neighbor tone, escape tone, anticipation.
narrative.emploiStep3: How is it approached? By step (passing tone, neighbor tone, suspension, stepwise appoggiatura) or by leap (appoggiatura, anticipation).
narrative.emploiStep4: Is it prepared? If yes, and on a strong beat with a downward resolution: it is a suspension.
narrative.emploiWarnBox: In realization: do not add non-chord tones at random. Each ornament must obey its rule of resolution, and the suspension must always be prepared. A poorly resolved appoggiatura or an unprepared suspension is penalized.
narrative.emploiP2: The reference repertoire remains the J.S. Bach chorale: within a few bars you find passing tones, neighbor tones, 4-3 and 7-6 suspensions, and pedal points — always led with rigor. It is the model to imitate for the DEM.
narrative.emploiGallonBox: A school of voice leading: alongside Théodore Dubois's Treatise, the teaching of Noël Gallon, professor of harmony at the Paris Conservatoire, is a benchmark for the use of non-chord tones. His work on harmonic sequences and voice leading reminds us that an ornament has value only through the melodic line it draws: every non-chord tone must serve the melody, never overload it.

cours39.title: Seventh Chords on Each Degree
cours39.subtitle: Beyond V7: the seventh chord on every scale degree — its quality, its preparation, its resolution, its inversions, and the chain of sevenths.

quiz.perfect: Perfect! You have mastered the quality, preparation, and resolution of seventh chords.
quiz.veryGood: Very good! A few points to consolidate (inversions, chain of sevenths).
quiz.keepGoing: Keep going — review the quality of each seventh and the downward resolution before starting again.

narrative.maitreAnecdote: Arcangelo Corelli, master of the Roman violin and father of the Baroque sonata, set the handling of dissonance for entire generations. His Sonatas and Concerti grossi overflow with long chains of sevenths that are prepared, struck, and resolved with clockwork regularity — the famous "Corelli cadence." Each seventh is announced as a consonance before becoming a dissonance, then falls by a step: a model of voice leading that Bach, and every treatise after him, would take up without question.
narrative.maitreLesson: A seventh is never a gratuitous effect: it is prepared, it is struck, then it descends to resolve. This discipline is what makes dissonance musical.
narrative.degIntro: Until now, the only seventh studied was the V7, the dominant seventh chord. But by stacking three thirds on any degree of the scale, we obtain a seventh chord. There are therefore seven of them (I7 to VII7), and they do not all have the same quality: it all depends on the intervals the scale makes available.
narrative.degInfoBox: Seventh chords on each degree are all the seventh chords other than the V7. Each belongs to an interval species (major seventh, minor seventh, half-diminished seventh, diminished seventh) according to the degree on which it is built.
narrative.degClickHint: Click a degree to hear it and read its quality.
narrative.degMineurBox: In minor mode: the qualities change. The VII of the harmonic scale (with leading tone) gives a diminished seventh (VII°7) — a stack of three minor thirds, a color of intense tension — instead of the half-diminished seventh of major. The second degree becomes half-diminished (IIø7), and the VI a major seventh. Always check the quality against the scale of the actual key.
narrative.ecrSectionTitle: Writing sevenths: preparation, resolution, inversions, the chain
narrative.ecrIntro: Every seventh is a dissonance: it therefore obeys precise rules of writing. In strict harmony, it is prepared, it is resolved downward, and its figuring is known in each inversion. Chained together, these sevenths form the famous chain of sevenths.
narrative.ecrPrepText: Preparing the seventh means sounding the note that will become dissonant first as a consonant note, in the same voice, in the preceding chord. The dissonance is thus "announced" to the ear before it strikes. Only the V7 is an exception: its dominant function allows its seventh to enter without preparation.
narrative.ecrResolBox: Resolution: the seventh almost always resolves by descending one step. It "falls" onto the lower neighbor note, which belongs to the following chord. Typical example: in V7 → I (C major), the seventh F descends to E, while the leading tone B rises to C.
narrative.ecrResolListenBtn: ▶ Listen to V7 → I (the seventh F descends to E)
narrative.ecrInvText: A seventh chord contains four notes: it therefore admits four positions. Each inversion corresponds to a classical figuring, inherited from the figured bass. In the third inversion, it is the dissonant seventh itself that lies in the bass — it will then have to descend in order to resolve.
narrative.ecrMarcheText: The chain of sevenths is a progression in which each chord is a seventh and the roots descend by fifths (descending circle of fifths). The seventh of one chord prepares the seventh of the next: it is a mechanism of linked preparations and resolutions, a model of writing par excellence.
narrative.ecrMarcheBox: Circle of fifths: I7 – IV7 – VII7 – III7 – VI7 – II7 – V7 – I. To lead the voices without errors, one alternates complete chords (4 notes) and incomplete ones (without the fifth): in this way the seventh of each chord is always prepared by a consonance of the preceding chord.
narrative.ecrMarcheListenBtn: ▶ Listen to a chain of sevenths (descending fifths)
narrative.ecrVoixBox: Voice leading: never double the seventh nor let it rise. Each seventh descends, each leading tone rises. It is the alternation of complete / incomplete chords that avoids parallel fifths in the chain — a rule already observed in Corelli and Bach.
narrative.quizFeedbackExcellent: Perfect! You have mastered the quality, preparation, and resolution of seventh chords.
narrative.quizFeedbackGood: Very good! A few points to consolidate (inversions, chain of sevenths).
narrative.quizFeedbackKeepGoing: Keep going — review the quality of each seventh and the downward resolution before starting again.

cours40.title: The Two-Voice Invention
cours40.subtitle: Subject, imitation at the octave, countersubject, episodes and tonal plan — writing the invention in Bach's style, toward the DEM counterpoint.

quiz.perfect: Perfect! You've mastered the subject, imitation at the octave and the tonal plan of the invention.
quiz.veryGood: Very good! A few points to consolidate (motivic devices, episodes in sequence).
quiz.keepGoing: Keep going — review the role of the subject, the imitation and the episodes before trying again.

narrative.maitreAnecdote: In 1723, Johann Sebastian Bach gathered fifteen short pieces (BWV 772–786) which he called “Inventions” for the use of his pupils — foremost among them his son Wilhelm Friedemann. In the preface he set a twofold aim: to learn to play cleanly in two voices, but above all to “have good ideas (inventiones) and to develop them.” Each invention grows from a single motive: a cell of a few notes that Bach imitates, inverts, fragments and carries through the neighbouring keys. The whole art lies in this economy — to bring forth an entire piece from a tiny idea.
narrative.maitreLesson: An invention is a motive brought to life: you state it, imitate it at the octave, develop it in episodes, and bring it back to the tonic. A single well-chosen subject is enough to build the whole piece.
narrative.structSectionTitle: The subject, the imitation and the countersubject
narrative.structIntro: A two-voice invention is a contrapuntal piece in which two independent voices converse from a single motive, the subject. It is the direct ancestor of the fugue and the first step toward the Bach counterpoint required at the DEM.
narrative.structInfoBox: The principle: all the material of the piece — melody, accompaniment, transitions — derives from a single motive. Nothing is gratuitous: every figure is a transformation of the subject or its countersubject.
narrative.structSujetText: The subject is the opening melodic motive. Its head-motive (incipit) — its first notes, its rhythm and its characteristic contour — gives the piece its recognisable identity. A good subject is short and memorable.
narrative.structImitText: The second voice repeats the subject after a short delay. Most often the imitation is at the octave (sometimes at the fifth). It is the imitation that creates the two-voice dialogue.
narrative.structImitBox: At the octave: the answer faithfully reproduces the subject, an octave lower (or higher). At the fifth, we approach the subject/answer principle of the fugue, but the invention most often stays at the octave.
narrative.structImitListenBtn: ▶ Listen to subject + imitation at the octave
narrative.structCsText: While the 2nd voice states the imitated subject, the 1st voice continues with a countersubject — a recurring melodic figure that accompanies the subject — or with a free voice. The two voices must remain genuinely independent (counterpoint).
narrative.structCsBox: Independence of the voices: the countersubject must not merely double the subject in chords. It converses with it: complementary rhythms, contrary motion, staggered entries. Therein lies the whole difficulty of two-voice counterpoint.
narrative.devSectionTitle: Episodes, motivic devices, tonal plan and method
narrative.devIntro: Once the subject has been stated and imitated, the invention unfolds: episodes provide the links and the modulations, while the motive is constantly developed through a series of devices. The whole follows a precise tonal plan.
narrative.devEpisodesText: The episodes (or divertimenti) are linking passages based on fragments of the motive. They are often treated as a sequence: a fragment is repeated at successive pitches, which causes modulation and leads from one key to the next.
narrative.devProcedesClickHint: Click on a device to hear it (motive then its transformation).
narrative.devStretteBox: The stretto: a very close imitation in which the 2nd voice enters before the 1st has finished stating the subject. The entries overlap, tightening the texture. In an invention one mostly encounters light strettos; it becomes systematic in the fugue.
narrative.devPlanText: An invention is not a free succession: it follows a tonal course. In major: exposition in the tonic, modulation to the dominant, development through the neighbouring keys, then return to the tonic to conclude.
narrative.devPlanMineurBox: In the minor mode: after the exposition, the modulation most often goes to the relative major (key of the third degree), sometimes to the minor dominant — the equivalent of the tonic → dominant move in major.
narrative.devMethodeTitle: Method: composing a short invention (8 to 16 bars)
narrative.devMethodeIntro: Four steps to build a complete invention from scratch:
narrative.devMethodeStep1: Invent a short, characteristic subject (a few notes with a recognisable contour and rhythm).
narrative.devMethodeStep2: Imitate it at the octave in the second voice, accompanying the imitated subject with a countersubject (or a free voice).
narrative.devMethodeStep3: Build episodes in sequence on fragments of the motive, modulating to the dominant and then to the neighbouring keys.
narrative.devMethodeStep4: Bring the subject back to the tonic and conclude with a perfect cadence (concluding cadence; intermediate cadences punctuate the tonal articulations).
narrative.devGedalgeBox: Toward the DEM: the rigour of motivic work — subject, countersubject, entries, sequences — is codified by André Gédalge in his Traité de la fugue (1901). Mastering the two-voice invention prepares directly for the DEM counterpoint test in Bach's style.
narrative.quizFeedbackExcellent: Perfect! You've mastered the subject, imitation at the octave and the tonal plan of the invention.
narrative.quizFeedbackGood: Very good! A few points to consolidate (motivic devices, episodes in sequence).
narrative.quizFeedbackKeepGoing: Keep going — review the role of the subject, the imitation and the episodes before trying again.

cours41.title: Stylistic Writing
cours41.subtitle: Pastiching the Classical, the Romantic and the early 20th century — the stylistic-writing discipline of the DEM (specialised cycle / CPES): identify the signatures of a style, then reproduce them.

quiz.perfect: Perfect! You unhesitatingly identify the signatures of the Classical, the Romantic and the early 20th century.
quiz.veryGood: Very good! A few stylistic traits still need refining (Romantic altered chords, Debussyan planing).
quiz.keepGoing: Keep going — review the signatures of each style before attempting the pastiche.

narrative.maitreAnecdote: At the Paris Conservatoire, Maurice Ravel was the most subtle pasticheur of his generation: his Menuet sur le nom d'Haydn or his À la manière de… (Borodin, Chabrier) reveal a composer able to adopt another's writing down to its smallest harmonic mannerisms. To reproduce a style is not to copy it: it is to have taken apart its mechanisms — harmonic language, rhythm, texture, voice leading — to the point of being able to write fresh “Mozart” or “Brahms”. That is exactly what the DEM's stylistic-writing unit demands.
narrative.maitreLesson: To write in a style is first to analyse it: you spot the signatures of a model (harmony, harmonic rhythm, texture, voice leading, cadences), then reproduce them — without ever committing an anachronism.
narrative.stylesIntro: Stylistic writing — pastiche — is a central discipline of the DEM in writing (specialised cycle / CPES, e.g. at the CRR of Lyon). You are asked to harmonise a given melody or to complete a page in the language of a precise era. Each style has its signatures: a recognisable way of treating harmony, rhythm, texture and voice leading.
narrative.stylesInfoBox: The principle: you can only pastiche well what you have analysed. Before writing, you identify the characteristic traits of the target style; you then reproduce them faithfully, without mixing in devices from another era.
narrative.stylesClickHint: Click a style to expand its description and its signatures.
narrative.style_classique_desc: The Classical style aims for clarity. A given melody is readily harmonised for string quartet, in homophonic texture: a clear melody supported by a transparent accompaniment (often an Alberti bass). The harmony is diatonic, the cadences clear-cut.
narrative.style_classique_sig1: Clear homophonic texture (string quartet: 2 violins, viola, cello).
narrative.style_classique_sig3: Regular harmonic rhythm (chord change per beat or per bar).
narrative.style_romantique_desc: Romanticism intensifies expression. Chromaticism becomes pervasive, the harmony grows richer (ninths, modal borrowings, augmented sixths, altered chords). The voice leading thickens, tonality becomes ambiguous, and the phrasing calls for rubato.
narrative.style_xxe_desc: At the turn of the century, colour prevails over function. Composers turn to modality, to parallel chords (planing), to unresolved 9ths and 11ths, to the whole-tone scale and to pedals. A chord counts for its harmonic timbre more than for its tension.
narrative.stylesPiegeBox: The trap to avoid — harmonic anachronism: each device belongs to an era. A “Brahms-style” augmented sixth in a Mozart pastiche, or Debussyan planing on a Classical page, immediately “break” the style. It is the error most severely penalised at the DEM.
narrative.methodeSectionTitle: Pastiche method and annotated worked answers
narrative.methodeIntro: Pastiching is not improvising: it is a methodical process. You analyse a model, extract its signatures, then write by reproducing them faithfully. Here is the procedure.
narrative.methodeStepsTitle: The four steps of the pastiche
narrative.methodeStep1: Analyse a model from the target era: spot its harmonic language, its harmonic rhythm, its texture and its voice leading before writing a single note.
narrative.methodeStep2: List the signatures: which chords, which cadences, which texture, which devices characterise this style and set it apart from the others?
narrative.methodeStep3: Write by reproducing these signatures: harmonise the given melody (or complete the page) drawing only on the resources of the chosen language.
narrative.methodeStep4: Reread hunting for anachronisms: check that no device foreign to the era has slipped into the writing.
narrative.methodeAnachroBox: Harmonic anachronism, once again: it is the no. 1 trap of the pastiche. Reproducing a style requires a negative discipline — forbidding yourself everything that does not belong to the era (no whole-tone scale in Mozart, no Alberti bass in Debussy).
narrative.corrigesTitle: Annotated worked answers — one example per style
narrative.corrigesIntro: On one and the same given melody, here is how the chosen language shapes the realisation. Each worked answer highlights the signatures to reproduce.
narrative.corrige_classique_text: The given melody is harmonised for string quartet in homophonic texture. Diatonic harmony, regular harmonic rhythm (one chord per strong beat), accompaniment in Alberti bass in the second violin and viola. The phrase closes on a perfect cadence, prepared by a II–V–I or a half cadence at the midpoint. No superfluous chromaticism: clarity prevails.
narrative.corrige_romantique_text: The same melody is coloured with chromaticism: ninth chords, borrowings from the minor mode, an augmented sixth leading to the dominant. The voice leading becomes dense, with expressive inner lines; a resolution is delayed to create tonal ambiguity. The phrasing, ample, implies rubato.
narrative.corrige_xxe_text: Here harmony becomes colour: one uses parallel chords (planing) of ninths, a coloured pedal in the bass, modal inflections (e.g. Dorian or Mixolydian) and, occasionally, the whole-tone scale. The dissonances do not resolve: they sound for themselves. Tonal function fades in favour of sensation.
narrative.methodeDemBox: Towards the DEM: the stylistic-writing unit (DEM / CPES cursus, e.g. CRR of Lyon) assesses precisely this ability to write “in the style of”. Practising the three languages — Classical, Romantic, early 20th century — and banishing anachronism is the key to the exam.
narrative.quizFeedbackExcellent: Perfect! You unhesitatingly identify the signatures of the Classical, the Romantic and the early 20th century.
narrative.quizFeedbackGood: Very good! A few stylistic traits still need refining (Romantic altered chords, Debussyan planing).
narrative.quizFeedbackKeepGoing: Keep going — review the signatures of each style before attempting the pastiche.

cours1.intuition: Before any theory, the scale is a physical experience — feeling the tension of semitones and the resolution toward the tonic.
cours1.citation: The scale is the musician's alphabet; without it, no phrase can be written.
cours1.voix0: Diatonic semitones (E–F, B–C) create the fundamental harmonic tensions
cours1.voix1: Each scale degree has a function: tonic, dominant, subdominant
cours1.voix2: The leading tone (VII) must rise to the tonic in the soprano voice
cours1.piege0erreur: Using the natural minor scale at the dominant
cours1.piege0correction: Always raise the leading tone (VII#) in a minor harmonic context
cours1.resume2: Greek modes derived from rotations of the major scale

cours2.intuition: A chord is not a stack of notes but a tension between them — understanding intervals means hearing harmony before reading it.
cours2.citation: Every chord has a personality determined by the intervals it contains.
cours2.voix0: The dominant chord (V) contains the tritone B–F that resolves to the tonic chord
cours2.voix1: Parallel fifths and octaves between voices are forbidden
cours2.voix2: Double the root in root position, the third in first inversion
cours2.piege0erreur: Doubling the third of the dominant chord (leading tone)
cours2.piege0correction: Double the root or the fifth, never the leading tone
cours2.resume1: Major triad: major third + perfect fifth

cours3.intuition: Harmonic function is the dramaturgical role of each chord in the musical discourse — tonic rests, dominant tensions, subdominant prepares.
cours3.citation: Harmony is not an end in itself, but a means of expression.
cours3.voix0: The authentic progression V–I is the cadence par excellence
cours3.voix1: The plagal cadence IV–I concludes gently (amen cadence)
cours3.voix2: Avoid bass movements by unresolved tritone
cours3.piege0correction: Respect tonal direction: T → S → D → T
cours3.resume0: Functions: Tonic (I, III, VI), Dominant (V, VII), Subdominant (II, IV)
cours3.resume2: The tritone in V7 resolves: B→C, F→E

cours4.intuition: The cadence is the punctuation of musical discourse — a comma, a semicolon, a period depending on its type.
cours4.badge: Aldwell & Schachter · Harmony and Voice Leading
cours4.citation: Cadences articulate musical form at every level, from the phrase to the whole composition.
cours4.voix0: The perfect cadence (V–I) requires the root in both the bass AND the soprano
cours4.voix1: The imperfect cadence (V–I with third in soprano) concludes less definitively
cours4.voix2: The deceptive cadence (V–VI) thwarts expectation — expressive use
cours4.piege0erreur: Placing a deceptive cadence at the end of a piece
cours4.piege0correction: The deceptive cadence restarts the discourse — reserve the perfect cadence for conclusions
cours4.resume0: 4 cadences: perfect, imperfect, half cadence (I–V), deceptive (V–VI)
cours4.resume1: Perfect cadence = tonic chord in root position
cours4.resume2: Cadence structures form: period, section, movement

cours5.intuition: Modal borrowing is the chiaroscuro of tonal harmony — a foreign note that colors without breaking the tonality.
cours5.citation: Borrowing is an aborted modulation that enriches the harmonic palette without leaving the key.
cours5.voix0: The Neapolitan sixth chord (bII in 1st inversion) prepares the dominant
cours5.voix1: Minor-mode chords (bVI, bVII) are frequently borrowed in major
cours5.voix2: Resolve chromatic alterations in the direction of their tension
cours5.piege0correction: Every borrowed note creates a tension that must resolve in the affected voices
cours5.resume0: Borrowing = chord from a parallel or relative key
cours5.resume2: Modal mixture: major chords on naturally minor degrees

cours6.intuition: Non-harmonic tones are the spice of harmony — they create the dissonance that makes consonance desirable.
cours6.citation: Non-harmonic tones are the spice of musical texture; used with discretion they add variety and forward motion.
cours6.voix0: The suspension is prepared consonant, struck dissonant, resolved downward
cours6.voix1: A tonic or dominant pedal can support foreign harmonies
cours6.voix2: Neighbor notes (auxiliary tones) never double the bass at their point of dissonance
cours6.piege0correction: A suspension always resolves by downward motion (except rare Baroque exceptions)
cours6.resume0: Non-harmonic tones: passing, neighbor, suspension, anticipation, escape, cambiata
cours6.resume2: Pedal point: sustained note under changing harmonies

cours7.intuition: The secondary dominant is a ray of sunlight from another key — it momentarily tonicizes a degree without truly modulating.
cours7.citation: Secondary dominants temporarily tonicize scale degrees, creating harmonic color without full modulation.
cours7.voix0: V/V (dominant of the dominant) heightens the authentic cadence
cours7.voix1: Every degree except VII can be preceded by its secondary dominant
cours7.voix2: Secondary dominants introduce chromatic alterations that must resolve
cours7.piege0erreur: Applying V/IV in major (produces an unexpected minor IV chord)
cours7.piege0correction: Check the quality of the tonicized chord before applying the secondary dominant
cours7.resume1: Alterations always resolved in the chromatic direction
cours7.resume2: Common in jazz and Romantic-era music

cours8.intuition: The pivot chord is the bridge between two keys — the same chord, two functions, an imperceptible transition.
cours8.badge: Aldwell & Schachter · Harmony and Voice Leading
cours8.citation: The pivot chord belongs to both keys simultaneously, making the modulation smooth and logical.
cours8.voix0: Choose the pivot from among the chords common to both keys
cours8.voix1: Confirm the new key with an authentic cadence after the pivot
cours8.voix2: Modulation to neighboring keys (±1 sharp/flat) is the smoothest
cours8.piege0erreur: Using a diminished chord as a pivot (too much ambiguity)
cours8.piege0correction: Prefer major or minor chords as pivot to guarantee tonal clarity
cours8.resume0: Pivot chord = common to both the departing and arriving key
cours8.resume1: Closely related modulations: relative, parallel, fifth above/below
cours8.resume2: Always confirm with a cadence in the new key

cours9.intuition: The harmonic sequence is the repetition of a melodic-harmonic pattern at regular intervals — it creates an internal logic perceptible even without theoretical knowledge.
cours9.citation: Sequences are the most powerful tool of tonal development; they generate both expectation and surprise.
cours9.voix0: The harmonic sequence transposes the pattern at the same interval quality
cours9.voix1: Tonal sequences stay in key; real sequences leave it
cours9.piege0erreur: Writing a real sequence while keeping all the accidentals of the original key
cours9.piege0correction: A real sequence transposes intervals exactly; accidentals change according to the degree
cours9.resume0: Tonal sequence: transposition in the same key (diatonic intervals)
cours9.resume1: Real sequence: exact transposition (identical chromatic intervals)
cours9.resume2: Chromaticism: non-diatonic alterations enriching harmony without modulating

satb.noError: No errors in the notes placed so far.
satb.complete: Progression complete — no harmonic errors found.
